sources.timestamp: 1mo •
sources.text:
NASA figured out how to write bulletproof code in 2006 and almost nobody used it.

Gerard Holzmann at JPL published the "Power of Ten," ten rules for safety-critical software. Simple stuff: every function fits on one printed page, every loop has a fixed upper bound, no dynamic memory after initialization, minimum two assertions per function. He kept it to ten rules because he'd watched organizations write 100+ page coding standards that developers just ignored.

The problem was always enforcement. You could put these rules on a wiki and tell people to follow them, but verifying compliance across hundreds of thousands of lines of code required either expensive tooling or a very patient human reviewer. Neither scaled well outside of JPL.

I think about this a lot, actually. Not just the Power of Ten specifically, but this whole category of things: ideas that were clearly right but too expensive to enforce. Checklists that worked in theory but fell apart in practice. The gap between knowing what good looks like and actually doing it consistently.

That gap is what AI agents collapse. You encode the rules, the agent applies them on every commit, every PR, every deployment. It doesn't have an opinion about whether the rules are worth following. It doesn't skip the check because it's been a long week.

I'm building agent workflows at Whimsey Labs right now, and honestly the hardest part isn't getting agents to follow rules. It's figuring out which rules are actually worth encoding. That's where the Power of Ten is helpful as a reference. Holzmann spent years figuring out which ten rules would give you the most coverage with the least friction. That kind of thinking transfers directly.

Still figuring out the velocity tradeoff. But the distance between "best practices exist" and "best practices get enforced" just shrank by a lot.

sources.text:
I'm starting a public experiment where I'm handing my entire codebase over to AI. No humans allowed to write or review a single line of code!

It's called a Dark Factory. And no, I wouldn't recommend it for reliable production code - yet. That's exactly why I'm calling it an experiment.

Dan Shapiro laid out five levels of AI coding autonomy earlier this year, using a self-driving car analogy. Level 3 is where reliable production AI coding lives right now: AI generates most of the code, but you're still reviewing every plan and every PR before it ships. That's what I teach in my Agentic Coding Course. You're in a self-driving car but still watching the road and ready to course correct if needed.

Level 5 is the Dark Factory - no steering wheel. Nothing gets reviewed. Even I'm a little uncomfortable with it! But this is where AI coding is heading, and nobody has run it as an open experiment before.

StrongDM did a version of this internally (32K lines shipped, zero human review, went viral in February), but you can't watch it work or participate. That's the gap I'm filling!

Here's how I'm building it, and this is where Archon (my open source harness builder) becomes the backbone.

Governance layer:

Three files loaded at the start of every workflow: MISSION.md (what the factory can build, what it must never touch), FACTORY_RULES.md (triage, PR size limits, quality gates, mandatory browser testing), CLAUDE.md (stack, layout, conventions). This is the only meaningful way I can stay aligned with a system that ships without asking me.

Archon workflows:

Archon is my open source harness builder - it turns your coding process into packaged up YAML workflows that can include both AI and deterministic steps. Three of them run the factory:

- Triage: classifies GitHub issues against MISSION.md + FACTORY_RULES.md
- Implement: runs in parallel for every accepted issue
- Validate: completely separate workflow to fully review PRs + merge

The validate workflow uses the "holdout" pattern from StrongDM. The validator only sees the user journey and the diff, zero context from development. No sycophancy, no rewriting tests to fake green.

If validation passes, it merges to main. No human approval at all.

The coding model is Minimax M2.7 routed into Claude Code via env vars. Purely an economics call, my Anthropic subscription would hit rate limits in hours at this throughput!

The app the factory is building is an AI Tutor, a RAG agent over my YouTube content. That part I'm genuinely excited about on its own.

Within a week or two it'll be deployed! You'll be able to file real GitHub issues and watch the factory accept, implement, validate, and ship them without me touching a line. Full architecture breakdown is on my YouTube channel!

Paste any GitHub URL and get a full interactive architecture map in seconds.

CodeFlow is a single HTML file that maps any GitHub repo's architecture.

Paste a URL or upload a project, get a full interactive dependency graph.

No install, no account, no build step.

Everything runs in your browser. Your code never touches a server.

Pick any file and see its blast radius.

That means every other file that would break if you changed it.

It also scans for security issues automatically.

Hardcoded API keys, SQL injection patterns, eval() calls, debug statements in production.

Here's what else it detects:

> Singleton, factory, and observer patterns
> Code ownership per file from git history
> Circular dependencies and dead code
> Commit frequency heatmaps across files

The whole thing ships as one HTML file. Clone and open index.html.

Works with private repos too.

Fully open source.

sources.text:
CoT, ReAct, ToT, RAG, MCP, DAG.

Most engineers building agents can't explain what these mean.

They copy-paste from tutorials and wonder why their agent loops forever.

Here's the AI Agent stack, decoded:

🔹 CoT (Chain of Thought)
Make the model think step by step before answering.
Without CoT, LLMs jump to conclusions. With it, they reason through problems.
The difference between "guess" and "derive."

🔹 ReAct (Reason + Act)
CoT plus tool use. The model reasons, then acts, then observes, then reasons again.
This is how agents actually work. Think → Do → See → Repeat.
Most "agents" skip the reasoning. That's why they fail.

🔹 ToT (Tree of Thoughts)
CoT explores one path. ToT explores many.
The model branches, evaluates, backtracks. Slower, but catches mistakes CoT misses.
Use it when the first answer is usually wrong.

🔹 RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
Don't make the model memorize everything. Fetch what it needs at runtime.
Vector search → relevant context → grounded answer.
Without RAG, your agent hallucinates. With it, your agent cites sources.

🔹 MCP (Model Context Protocol)
Anthropic's standard for connecting LLMs to external tools.
One protocol. Any tool. No custom integrations per service.
The USB-C of AI agents.

🔹 DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)
How you orchestrate multi-step workflows without infinite loops.
Task A → Task B → Task C. No cycles. Clear dependencies.
LangGraph, Prefect, Airflow — all DAGs under the hood.

The engineers who understand these don't just USE agent frameworks.

They understand WHY agents work.

Want to go deeper?

Full Single-Agent Patterns deep-dive  in comments 👇

💾 Bookmark this before your next agent breaks and you don't know why.

sources.text:
𝐂𝐥𝐚𝐮𝐝𝐞 𝐂𝐨𝐝𝐞 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐣𝐞𝐜𝐭 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞: 𝐄𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲 𝐅𝐢𝐥𝐞 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐅𝐨𝐥𝐝𝐞𝐫 𝐄𝐱𝐩𝐥𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐝

Most Claude Code projects start with a CLAUDE.md and nothing else.
Here is the full project structure that turns Claude from a coding assistant into an engineering partner.

𝐓𝐇𝐄 𝐂𝐎𝐌𝐏𝐋𝐄𝐓𝐄 𝐃𝐈𝐑𝐄𝐂𝐓𝐎𝐑𝐘

your-project/

1. CLAUDE.md
Loaded at session start. 
Defines project overview, tech stack, and commands. 
Contains coding conventions and architecture. 
Supports CLAUDE.local.md overrides for personal preferences without affecting the team.

2. CLAUDE.local.md
Your personal overrides. 
Keeps individual preferences separate from shared project context.

3. mcp.json
Stores MCP integration configs. 
Connects to GitHub, JIRA, Slack, databases. 
Shared across the team via git. This single file controls every external tool your agent can reach.

4. claude/settings.json
Controls permissions and tool access. 
Defines model selection and hooks. 
Supports settings.local.json overrides for individual developers.

5. claude/rules/
Modular .md files organized by topic. 
Covers style, testing, and API design. Can target specific files or paths.

• code-style.md: How code should look
• testing.md: Testing standards and patterns
• api-conventions.md: API design rules

Rules load contextually. Claude follows code-style.md when writing code, testing.md when generating tests.

6. claude/commands/
Custom slash commands (/project:name). 
Used for repeatable workflows. Supports shell execution.

• review.md: Code review workflow
• fix-issue.md: Issue resolution steps

Type /project:review and Claude runs your entire review process.

7. claude/skills/
Auto-triggered based on task context. 
Loads only when needed. 
Keeps context lightweight.

• deploy/SKILL.md: Deployment procedures
• deploy/deploy-config.md: Configuration details

Skills activate automatically when Claude detects a relevant task.

8. claude/agents/
Specialized sub-agents with roles. 
Isolated context windows. 
Custom tools and model preferences.

• code-reviewer.md: Dedicated code review agent
• security-auditor.md: Security-focused analysis agent

Each agent operates in its own context without polluting the main conversation.

9. claude/hooks/
Event-driven scripts (pre/post tool use). 
Automates validation, linting, and formatting. 
Blocks unsafe operations.

• validate-bash.sh: Validates bash commands before execution

Hooks are your guardrails. They run automatically before or after Claude takes action.

The quality of Claude Code's output is directly proportional to the quality of your project structure.

Invest in the structure once and every session benefits.

𝐖𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐝𝐨𝐞𝐬 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐂𝐥𝐚𝐮𝐝𝐞 𝐂𝐨𝐝𝐞 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐣𝐞𝐜𝐭 𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞 𝐥𝐨𝐨𝐤 𝐥𝐢𝐤𝐞 𝐭𝐨𝐝𝐚𝐲?

sources.text:
AI agents fail without strong memory layers.

5 key AI memory systems, explained simply.

1. 𝗦𝗵𝗼𝗿𝘁-𝗧𝗲𝗿𝗺 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 (𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗹-𝗧𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝘅𝘁)
𝗚𝗼𝗮𝗹: Handle 𝗹𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗶𝗺𝗺𝗲𝗱𝗶𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝘅𝘁.

𝗜𝗻𝗽𝘂𝘁 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴
• Receive user input
• Break into tokens
• Understand intent

𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝘅𝘁 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁
• Store recent messages
• Maintain session context
• Keep latest interactions

𝗕𝘂𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿 𝗛𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴
• Build temporary memory buffer
• Remove old context
• No long-term carryover

𝗢𝘂𝘁𝗽𝘂𝘁 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Send context to model
• Generate response
• Return reply
________________
2. 𝗟𝗼𝗻𝗴-𝗧𝗲𝗿𝗺 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 (𝗣𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗞𝗻𝗼𝘄𝗹𝗲𝗱𝗴𝗲)
𝗚𝗼𝗮𝗹: Remember and reuse knowledge across sessions.

𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗮𝗴𝗲
• Save important inputs
• Attach metadata
• Organize information

𝗘𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗱𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗖𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Convert data into vectors
• Enable similarity search
• Optimize retrieval

𝗥𝗲𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗲𝘃𝗮𝗹 𝗦𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺
• Search past memories
• Find relevant matches
• Rank useful results

𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Combine with current input
• Enhance responses
• Update memory
________________
3. 𝗘𝗽𝗶𝘀𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗰 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 (𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘁-𝗕𝗮𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴)
𝗚𝗼𝗮𝗹: Designed to 𝗰𝗮𝗽𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗽𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀.

𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗖𝗮𝗽𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲
• Identify important interactions
• Store detailed logs
• Track outcomes

𝗘𝗽𝗶𝘀𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗖𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Group related events
• Structure data
• Save as episodes

𝗣𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
• Analyze past behavior
• Detect patterns
• Improve decisions

𝗥𝗲𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗲𝘃𝗮𝗹
• Match similar past events
• Guide current actions
• Enhance outputs
________________
4. 𝗦𝗲𝗺𝗮𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 (𝗙𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗞𝗻𝗼𝘄𝗹𝗲𝗱𝗴𝗲)
𝗚𝗼𝗮𝗹: To 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗳𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘀, 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗽𝘁𝘀, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗿𝗲𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽𝘀.

𝗤𝘂𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴
• Detect user intent
• Convert into query
• Prepare search

𝗞𝗻𝗼𝘄𝗹𝗲𝗱𝗴𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗲𝘃𝗮𝗹
• Look up knowledge base
• Match relevant concepts
• Pull accurate facts

𝗥𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗼𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
• Connect ideas
• Validate information
• Add references

𝗢𝘂𝘁𝗽𝘂𝘁
• Format response
• Deliver structured answer
• Ensure clarity
________________
5. 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗰𝗲𝗱𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 (𝗧𝗮𝘀𝗸 𝗘𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻)
𝗚𝗼𝗮𝗹: To 𝗲𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗲 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗳𝗹𝗼𝘄𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 efficiently.

𝗧𝗮𝘀𝗸 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴
• Identify task type
• Define objective
• Map requirements

𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗶𝗻𝗴
• Break into steps
• Load workflows
• Assign actions

𝗘𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
• Perform step-by-step tasks
• Use tools & APIs
• Track progress

𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
• Record results
• Improve performance
• Optimize workflows

The future of AI systems depends on combining all these memory layers together.

✅ Repost for people in your network building AI systems & applications.

sources.text:
4 habits that make Claude Code more reliable in practice:

1---- Don’t turn CLAUDE. md into a giant instruction dump

Use it for stable things: project context, architecture notes, conventions, important commands.

If something is really a repeatable procedure, checklist, or playbook, it usually belongs somewhere else.

2---- Use Plan Mode before bigger changes

For multi-file edits, refactors, or anything slightly messy, it is usually safer to let Claude inspect and plan first before touching code.

3---- Turn repeated prompts into skills

If you keep typing the same “review this,” “debug this,” or “prepare this for deploy” instructions, that is usually a sign the workflow should become reusable instead of rewritten every time.

4---- Use hooks for things that should happen every time

Formatting, validation, guardrails, notifications - anything deterministic is better handled by the system than by hoping the model remembers.

The more useful shift, to me, is moving the right things out of the prompt and into the environment.

That’s where repeated prompting starts turning into structure.

If you want to learn Claude Code more deeply, we’re also running a live course and hands-on workshop on it:

This GitHub repo just hit 16k stars 🔥 It gives your AI agent eyes to see the entire internet:

Most agents are blind to the live web. They can reason and write code, but ask them to check a tweet or pull a YouTube transcript - they're stuck.

Agent-Reach fixes this. It's a Python CLI that connects your agent to the platforms where information actually lives:

• X
• Reddit
• YouTube
• GitHub
• Any webpage or RSS feed

No paid APIs. No subscriptions. Setup is one line.

It auto-detects your environment and tells you what's ready. Run `agent-reach doctor` anytime to see what's working and what's not.

Works with Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf - any agent that runs commands.

Link to the project: https://lnkd.in/dv-vvezp

🚨 Andrej Karpathy’s just shared his new set-up on 𝕏.

It’s literally a self-improving second brain… with zero manual editing 🤯

A living AI knowledge base that continuously fixes and refines itself.

Let me break it down.

Instead of relying on complex RAG, the LLM pulls raw research directly into an Obsidian Markdown wiki.

It completely takes over:

✦ Index creation
✦ System linting
✦ Native Q&A routing

The core process is beautifully simple:

→ You dump raw sources into a folder
→ The LLM auto-compiles an indexed .md wiki
→ You ask complex questions
→ It generates outputs (Marp slides, matplotlib plots) and files them back in

The big-picture implication of this is just wild.

When agents maintain their own memory layer, they don’t need massive, expensive context limits.

They really just need two things:

→ Clean file organization
→ The ability to query their own indexes

Forget stuffing everything into one giant prompt.

This approach is way cheaper, highly scalable... and 100% inspectable!

sources.text:
I built an AI second brain with Claude Code over the last three months. It saves me 12+ hours a week! But the part that actually changed how I work isn't the time savings.

It's that the system anticipates what I need before I think to ask.

Every conversation I have with it, every decision I make, every email it reads on my behalf - it all feeds into a memory layer that gets sharper over time. It learns how I communicate, what I prioritize, which tasks tend to slip. Three months in, it knows my patterns well enough to flag things I'd miss on my own!

The proactive piece (inspired by OpenClaw) is what makes it feel different from every other AI tool I've used. It runs in the background, monitoring my calendar, email, tasks, and Slack. But it doesn't just alert me. It acts. It drafts replies to emails I haven't gotten to, handles routine tasks in Asana, catches scheduling conflicts before they become problems. When something needs my judgment, it surfaces it. When it can handle something on its own, it just does.

I don't open the system and ask questions. The system is already working before I sit down.

And because the whole thing compounds, the actions get smarter. It's not matching against static rules. It's reasoning with context about my work, my priorities, how things went last time. The decisions it makes today are meaningfully better than the ones it made even a few weeks ago.

That's the gap between a tool you use and a system that works for you. The tool sits there until you remember it exists. The system is already thinking about your day.

I just posted a full breakdown on YouTube walking through every component of the second brain, how the proactive layer works, and what three months of building this looks like end to end.

And this Friday (April 4 at 9:00 AM CST) I'm running a live workshop in the Dynamous community (link in my bio) where you build the whole thing from scratch. Eight modules, starting from zero, leaving with a working second brain on your machine!

sources.author: Cole Medin
sources.timestamp:
2w • 
 
2 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn
sources.text:
The biggest problem in AI coding right now is sycophancy.

Not in the broad "AI agrees with everything you say" sense, though that's real. Specifically this: when you have a coding agent evaluate its own work, it's a student grading its own homework. It'll flag a couple of minor issues to make the review look credible, but the deeper problems get stuffed under the rug.

The model was trained to agree. That bias doesn't disappear just because you asked it to be critical!

So the fix is architecture, not "prompting harder".

I built what I call an adversarial dev harness, inspired by how Anthropic is thinking about long-running coding agents. The core idea comes from GANs (generative adversarial networks), where a generator and a discriminator are trained against each other. Here, the generator writes code. The evaluator's sole job is to find flaws, not approve.

The architecture looks like this:

User prompt goes to a Planner, which expands it into a full spec. Then you enter a sprint cycle. Before a single line of code is written, the Generator and Evaluator negotiate the evaluation criteria together. They agree up front on what each sprint looks like and how it gets scored on a 1-10 scale. Only then does the Generator implement, and the Evaluator rips it apart against those pre-negotiated criteria.

Two core principles at work here:

1. Separate evaluation from coding entirely (different agents, isolated contexts, no shared bias)
2. Scope and contract before implementation (criteria set before code is written, not after)

The results are legitimately incredible. I used this harness to one-shot a full-stack RAG app with a polished chat UI, token streaming, source citations, the whole thing. Ran for about four hours. And I used Claude Sonnet 4.6 for it, not Opus (huge difference in capability)!

That's the real leverage: a better harness lets you use a cheaper, faster model and still get better output than a stronger model running alone.

I posted a full breakdown on YouTube (link to my channel in my bio) with code walkthroughs for both the Claude Code and Codex versions of the harness, plus a live demo of the RAG app it built.

sources.text:
Someone just open-sourced the "Docker for AI agents" 🤯

Define your agent once. Run it in Claude Code, OpenAI, CrewAI, LangChain, Google Gemini CLI, and OpenClaw.

Meet GitAgent. Your repo is the agent.

Right now every framework defines agents differently. Your CrewAI agent can't run in Claude Code. Your LangChain agent can't port to OpenAI.

You want to switch frameworks? You rewrite everything. Prompts, tools, memory, config. All of it. Again.

That's not a feature of the ecosystem. That's vendor lock-in wearing an open-source badge.

GitAgent replaces all of it with a standard folder structure.

agent.yaml for config. SOUL.md for personality. RULES.md for hard constraints. skills/ for capabilities. tools/ for integrations. memory/ for persistence.

Two files are required. Everything else is optional.

Then one command exports to any runtime.

Same repo runs in Claude Code, OpenAI Agents SDK, CrewAI, LangGraph, Google Gemini CLI, OpenClaw, and Cursor.

No rewrite. No adapter code. No framework-specific config.

Git becomes the supervision layer. Every prompt change is a commit. Every rollback is a checkout. Every agent review is a PR. Agent quality gets treated like code quality.

What makes this more than a packaging spec:
• Built-in segregation of duties for regulated industries. Define maker, checker, and auditor roles. 
• Sub-agents compose like submodules. Fork a public agent, swap its SOUL.md, plug in your own tools.
• CI/CD runs gitagent validate on every push. Bad agent configs don't ship.

Dockerfiles didn't replace container runtimes. They gave developers one format that every runtime could read.

GitAgent is that moment for agents.

Link to the GitHub repo in the comments.

sources.author: Michael Ulin
sources.timestamp:
1mo • 
 
1 month ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn
sources.text:
NASA figured out how to write bulletproof code in 2006 and almost nobody used it.

Gerard Holzmann at JPL published the "Power of Ten," ten rules for safety-critical software. Simple stuff: every function fits on one printed page, every loop has a fixed upper bound, no dynamic memory after initialization, minimum two assertions per function. He kept it to ten rules because he'd watched organizations write 100+ page coding standards that developers just ignored.

The problem was always enforcement. You could put these rules on a wiki and tell people to follow them, but verifying compliance across hundreds of thousands of lines of code required either expensive tooling or a very patient human reviewer. Neither scaled well outside of JPL.

I think about this a lot, actually. Not just the Power of Ten specifically, but this whole category of things: ideas that were clearly right but too expensive to enforce. Checklists that worked in theory but fell apart in practice. The gap between knowing what good looks like and actually doing it consistently.

That gap is what AI agents collapse. You encode the rules, the agent applies them on every commit, every PR, every deployment. It doesn't have an opinion about whether the rules are worth following. It doesn't skip the check because it's been a long week.

I'm building agent workflows at Whimsey Labs right now, and honestly the hardest part isn't getting agents to follow rules. It's figuring out which rules are actually worth encoding. That's where the Power of Ten is helpful as a reference. Holzmann spent years figuring out which ten rules would give you the most coverage with the least friction. That kind of thinking transfers directly.

Still figuring out the velocity tradeoff. But the distance between "best practices exist" and "best practices get enforced" just shrank by a lot.

sources.author: Cole Medin
sources.timestamp:
1mo • 
 
1 month ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn
sources.text:
After 2,000+ hours using Claude Code across real production codebases, I can tell you the thing that separates reliable from unreliable isn't the model, the prompt, or even the task complexity.

It's context management.

About 80% of the coding agent failures I see trace back to poor context - either too much noise, the wrong information loaded at the wrong time, or context that's drifted from the actual state of the codebase. Even with a 1M token window, Chroma's research shows that performance degrades as context grows. More tokens is not always better.

I built the WISC framework (inspired by Anthropic's research) to handle this systematically.

Four strategy areas:

W - Write (externalize your agent's memory)
- Git log as long-term memory with standardized commit messages
- Plan in one session, implement in a fresh one
- Progress files and handoffs for cross-session state

I - Isolate (keep your main context clean)
- Subagents for research (90.2% improvement per Anthropic's data)
- Scout pattern to preview docs before committing them to main context

S - Select (just in time, not just in case)
- Global rules (always loaded)
- On-demand context for specific code areas
- Skills with progressive disclosure
- Prime commands for live codebase exploration

C - Compress (only when you have to)
- Handoffs for custom session summaries
- /compact with targeted summarization instructions

These work on any codebase. Not just greenfield side projects! I've applied this on enterprise codebases spanning multiple repositories, and the reliability improvement is consistent.

I also just published a YouTube video going over the WISC framework in a lot more detail. Very value packed! Check it out here:

THIS is the wildest open-source project I’ve seen this month.

We were all hyped about Andrej Karpathy's autoresearch project automating the experiment loop a few weeks ago.
(ICYMI → https://lnkd.in/eDQBJQEz)

But a bunch of folks just took it ten steps further and automated the entire scientific method end-to-end.

It's called AutoResearchClaw, and it's fully open-source.

You pass it a single CLI command with a raw idea, and it completely takes over 🤯

The 23-stage loop they designed is insane:

✦ First, it handles the literature review.
- It searches arXiv and Semantic Scholar for real papers 
- Cross-references them against DataCite and CrossRef. 
- No fake papers make it through.

✦ Second, it runs the sandbox.
- It generates the code from scratch.
- If the code breaks, it self-heals. 
- You don't have to step in.

✦ Finally, it writes the paper.
- It structures 5,000+ words into Introduction, Related Work, Method, and Experiments. 
- It formats the math, generates the comparison charts,
- Then wraps the whole thing in official ICML or ICLR LaTeX templates.

You can set it to pause for human approval, or you can just pass the --auto-approve flag and walk away.

What it spits out at the end:
→ Full academic paper draft
→ Conference-grade .tex files
→ Verified, hallucination-free citations
→ All experiment scripts and sandbox results

This is what autonomous AI agents actually look like in 2026.

→ https://lnkd.in/eX_VXkDZ

Massive kudos to the team for making this open-source 🤗

NVIDIA just dropped a FREE 120B reasoning model.

Only 12B active during inference.

450 tokens per second. 3x faster than Qwen3.5

Open-source reasoning models were supposed to be slow and expensive.

Here's why that argument just died:

1️⃣ Latent Mixture-of-Experts
Traditional models activate ALL parameters for every token.
Nemotron 3 Super routes to specialized experts.
120B knowledge base. 12B compute cost.

2️⃣ Hybrid Mamba-Transformer
Mamba layers handle long sequences in linear time.
Transformer layers handle complex reasoning.
Best of both worlds in one model.

3️⃣ Multi-Token Prediction
Standard LLMs predict one token at a time.
This predicts 3 tokens per step.
That's where the 450 TPS comes from.

But the feature I care about most:

You can turn reasoning ON and OFF.

◦ enable_thinking: True → deep reasoning chains for hard problems
◦ enable_thinking: False → fast direct responses for simple tasks
◦ low_effort: True → somewhere in between

One model. Three modes. You control the compute.

Open weights. Open datasets (10T curated tokens)

You can run it on consumer hardware (NVFP4 on DGX Spark).

The Nemotron 3 family:
1️⃣ Nano (30B-A3B) → available Dec. 2025

sources.text:
Garry Tan, CEO of Y Combinator, just shared his Claude Code setup.

4,000+ lines of code. Full tests. Under 1 hour.

Here's what makes his approach DIFFERENT:

He doesn't ask Claude to write code first.

He asks it to review the plan. Like a staff engineer would before any changes are made.

One prompt forces Claude through a 4-stage structured review:

1️⃣ Architecture
System design, component boundaries, dependency graphs, scaling risks, security holes.

2️⃣ Code Quality
DRY violations flagged aggressively. Error handling gaps. Technical debt hotspots. Over-engineered vs under-engineered areas called out.

3️⃣ Tests
Coverage gaps across unit, integration, e2e. Missing edge cases. Untested failure modes. "Too many tests > too few."

4️⃣ Performance
N+1 queries. Memory usage. Caching opportunities. High-complexity paths.

For EVERY issue found, Claude must:

⚡ Describe the problem with file and line references
⚡ Present 2-3 options including "do nothing"
⚡ Specify effort, risk, impact, and maintenance burden
⚡ Make an opinionated recommendation
⚡ Ask for approval before proceeding

Two modes built in:

BIG CHANGE: full review, max 4 issues per section
SMALL CHANGE: one focused question per section

His 5 engineering preferences baked into the prompt:

1️⃣ DRY is non-negotiable
2️⃣ Well-tested code is non-negotiable
3️⃣ "Engineered enough" — not hacky, not over-abstracted
4️⃣ Handle MORE edge cases, not fewer
5️⃣ Explicit over clever. Thoughtfulness over speed.

The real insight:

Review before implementation. Tradeoffs before code. Feedback before action.

For small teams without a senior engineer on every PR: design the review process into your AI.

Are you still letting Claude Code skip the review? 👇

1mo • 
 
1 month ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn
sources.text:
Anthropic quietly showed one of the smartest ways to make Claude better over time.

It is not a new model.
It is one markdown file.

Boris Cherny, the creator of Claude Code, shared part of how their team works internally:

They use a CLAUDE.md file.
You write your rules once.
 Claude reads them before every session.
 Your workflow becomes consistent.
 Your corrections start compounding.

Here’s the setup:

1. For Claude Code
Create a file called CLAUDE.md
 Put it inside your project folder.
Claude uses it as persistent context for how you want work done.
That means you can tell it things like:
✦ Always plan before writing code
 ✦ For tasks with multiple steps, write a short spec first
 ✦ Run tests before marking anything complete
 ✦ Check logs before claiming a fix works
 ✦ Avoid quick hacks when a cleaner solution exists
 ✦ Document repeated mistakes so they do not happen again

This is where it gets powerful:
 Week 1: you correct Claude a few times
 Week 4: it starts following your workflow naturally
 Week 12: it feels less like a tool and more like a real teammate

2. For your personal workflow
You can do something similar with a profile.md file.
Use it to store:
✦ Your name, role, and priorities
 ✦ Your tone and writing preferences
 ✦ Your preferred output formats
 ✦ Things the AI should avoid
 ✦ Your active projects and current context

Instead of repeating yourself in every session,
 you give the AI a permanent operating manual.

That is the real lesson here:
The best AI users are not just prompting better.
They are building systems around the model.
One file will not make Claude magical overnight.

But one good context file, improved over time, can completely change how useful it becomes.

That is how AI stops feeling random
 and starts feeling operational.

sources.text:
Our new course, "Agentic AI Engineering," contains 17 notebooks and a complete multi-agent project (because you can't learn to build production agents through notebooks only).

Notebooks are really powerful for teaching the core concepts quickly and easily to anyone. Which we can then use in the final project. You don't have to set up your environment (using Colab), or you can do so very easily with specific requirement packages.

Still, we also believe it's important to "sweat" in order to learn, and notebooks are thus not the entirety of the course. The main goal of the course is to build your own production-grade agent systems (deployed to the cloud with Docker and Cloud Run, managed with CI/CD) by leveraging the techniques we teach through notebooks.

We teach every core technique in those notebooks. Each one has a dedicated course lesson, which serves a specific purpose through the applied project.

We keep it super practical by teaching throughout the course to replicate our very own Research Agent and Writing Workflow, giving all the techniques we've ever tried (including some we didn't keep in the final version but that can be relevant).

Then, the student's goal is to build and deploy their own MCP server, implementing the most advanced agent patterns, evaluation systems, and observability techniques, which becomes an amazing portfolio project.

If, after reading this, you feel it might be a course for you, check it out here: https://lnkd.in/eBCqSjMc
And the (free) notebooks here: https://lnkd.in/ecN7kMvK

sources.author: John George
sources.timestamp:
1mo • 
 
1 month ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn
sources.text:
Most people use Claude Code like a chatbot.

Describe what you want, get some code, fix the bits that are wrong, repeat. That works for small stuff. It falls apart on anything complex.

I use five slash commands that turn Claude Code into a structured engineering workflow.

/brainstorm is where I develop an idea. It explores the codebase, asks me questions, and proposes approaches with trade-offs. If the feature needs multiple tasks it writes a sprint with dependencies, so each task can be specced individually.

/spec-task produces functional requirements through iterative conversation. What to build, not how. The output is a self-contained document a completely fresh session can pick up and understand.

/plan-task consults expert subagents that research online documentation, API specs, local repos for dependent libraries, and examples I've built before. Then it writes a phased implementation plan with testing strategy and anti-patterns to avoid.

/implement-task executes phase by phase. At every phase boundary it updates the task docs, runs tests, and commits. When the context compacts (and it will), the next session reads those docs and picks up exactly where it left off.

/review-task audits the code against the spec. Mechanical check first, then expert subagent validation of every issue found.

None of this is radical. It's just software engineering. Specs before code. Plans before implementation. Reviews before merge. The only difference is Claude Code does most of the legwork.

sources.text:
Most think Claude Code = terminal + prompt.

That barely scratches the surface.

The real power of Claude Code isn’t the prompt.
It’s the system around the prompt.

Once you understand this, everything changes.

Claude Code actually works like a 4-layer AI engineering system:

1️⃣ CLAUDE.md → Project memory
Persistent context for your project.
Stores rules, conventions, and architecture decisions.

2️⃣ Skills → Reusable knowledge packs
Claude automatically invokes them when relevant.
Think: reusable workflows and expertise.

3️⃣ Hooks → Deterministic guardrails
Safety gates that run automatically.
Unlike prompts, hooks are 100% enforced.

4️⃣ Agents → Specialized sub-agents
Each with their own context window.
Great for complex, multi-step tasks.

Most engineers install Claude Code and immediately start prompting.

But the difference between average output and production-level results usually comes down to setup.

Practices that make a huge difference:

→ Run /init on day one to generate CLAUDE.md
→ Organize the .claude/ directory (skills, hooks, permissions)
→ Write clear skill descriptions so Claude knows when to trigger them
→ Use the memory hierarchy (global → project → folder)
→ Add hooks for critical rules (hooks = enforced, memory = advisory)

When structured properly, Claude Code stops feeling like a chatbot.

It starts behaving like a real AI development system.

If you want to learn it properly, Anthropic released several free courses:

Claude 101
https://lnkd.in/g6PiBcg5

https://lnkd.in/gEkvdXHX

Also includes developer topics like:

• Claude Code in Action

Bookmark this if you're building with Claude.

The future of AI development isn't just prompts.

It's systems around prompts.

sources.text:
Someone finally documented how to actually use Claude Code.

13K stars. claude-code-best-practice.

Direct from Boris Cherny and team:

→ Always use plan mode, give Claude a way to verify
→ Ask Claude to interview you using AskUserQuestion tool
→ Use Git Worktrees for parallel development
→ /loop - schedule recurring tasks for up to 3 days
→ Code Review - fresh context windows catch bugs the original agent missed
→ /btw - side chain conversations while Claude works
→ Make phase-wise gated plans with tests for each phase
→ Use cross-model (Claude Code + Codex) to review your plan
→ CLAUDE[.]md should target under 200 lines per file
→ Use commands for workflows instead of sub-agents
→ Have feature-specific sub-agents with skills instead of general QA or backend engineer
→ Vanilla Claude Code is better than complex workflows for smaller tasks
→ Take screenshots and share with Claude when stuck
→ Use MCP to let Claude see Chrome console logs
→ Ask Claude to run terminal as background task for better debugging
→ Use cross-model for QA - e.g. Codex for plan and implementation review

The community workflows included:
→ Cross-Model (Claude Code + Codex) Workflow
→ RPI (Research Plan Implement)
→ Ralph Wiggum Loop for autonomous tasks
→ Github Speckit (74K stars)
→ obra/superpowers (72K stars)
→ OpenSpec OPSX (28K stars)

The billion-dollar questions it addresses:
→ What should you put inside CLAUDE[.]md?
→ When should you use command vs agent vs skill?
→ Why does Claude ignore CLAUDE[.]md instructions?
→ Can we convert a codebase into specs and regenerate code from those specs alone?

The daily habits:
→ Update Claude Code daily
→ Start your day by reading the changelog
→ Follow r/ClaudeAI, r/ClaudeCode on Reddit

sources.text:
This tool analyzes your hardware with one command and shows which LLMs are actually worth running on your machine 👇

It's called llmfit - and it solves the most annoying part of running local AI.

Instead of downloading a model and hitting an OOM error, it scans your RAM, CPU, GPU, and VRAM first - then scores every model across 4 dimensions:

1. Quality - param count, model family, quantization penalty
2. Speed - estimated tok/s for your exact backend (CUDA, Metal, ROCm)
3. Fit - memory utilization vs. your available hardware
4. Context - context window vs. your use case

Each model gets a label: Perfect / Good / Marginal / Too Tight.

It also picks the best quantization automatically - starting at Q8_0, stepping down to Q2_K until something fits.

The detail I like most: it handles MoE architectures correctly. Mixtral 8x7B has 46.7B total params, but only ~12.9B are active per token. llmfit accounts for this, while many tools still miscalculate it.

Covers hundreds of models across major providers including Meta, Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, and more. Ollama integration built in.

14k+ stars. 100% open source. 
Link to the repo: https://lnkd.in/gpWPVS7g

sources.text:
I just deleted all my MCPs.
Skills + CLI is all you need.

MCPs were a solution for humans pretending to build for agents. But agents aren't humans. They're processes. And processes talk to the world through CLIs and APIs. Always have. Always will.

GitHub's official MCP server: 54,000 tokens to load the spec. Fifty-four thousand. Before I even ask it to do anything. 
Meanwhile, `gh --help` costs 562 tokens. And the model already knows it from training data.

MCP is a JSON bureaucracy pretending to be infrastructure. It's the enterprise middleware of AI, lots of ceremony, zero value add.

Skills are 30-50 tokens until triggered. Progressive disclosure. They teach the agent how to think. CLIs are the what- battle-tested, version-controlled, debuggable without a spec document.

Make sure you own your AI. AI in the cloud is not aligned with you; it’s aligned with the company that owns it.

sources.text:
All legacy codebase modernization efforts in 2026 begin with: “let’s give an agent the repos”
Which is a great way to let Claude Code, opencode, Copilot, and friends misunderstand 40 repositories at machine speed.

In real enterprise code estates, the architecture usually lives in:
one staff engineer’s head… who retired in 2024,
three geological eras of DI,
`HttpClient` chains crossing repo boundaries,
and EF Core mappings to tables named by ancient forces.

My agents use local AI with dede, an engineering R&D context system, short for `DogEatDog.DependencyExplorer`

It scans a workspace full of sibling repos and extracts a deterministic Roslyn-backed AST/semantic graph across controllers, services, methods, internal HTTP calls, EF Core lineage, and cross-repo dependencies.

Here is a live scan over 13 repos, 1,962 projects, and 52,800 methods.
Not regex theater. 
Not architecture fan fiction. 
Not LLM vibes.
Just 100% local AI and tooling.

The useful part is giving that graph thingy to AI coding agents as a skill.
Now agents can query compiler-backed structure instead of improvising:
“What breaks if I change this endpoint?” 
“Which tables are in the blast radius?” 
“Where does this service cross repo boundaries?” 
“Which edges are ambiguous and need a human?”

Make sure you own your AI. AI in the cloud is not aligned with you; it’s aligned with the company that owns it.

sources.author: Nikki Siapno
sources.timestamp:
1mo • Edited • 
 
1 month ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn
sources.text:
AI Feature vs AI-Native Platform: They are NOT the same.

If you’re unclear, read this:

The difference is about where the work happens: outside the product (suggestions) vs inside the workflow (actions).

That’s the shift happening right now:

AI that answers → AI that acts inside the platform.

Here's a simple breakdown (save this):

𝗖𝗵𝗮𝘁𝗯𝗼𝘁 𝗹𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿 (𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝗳𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹)
 ↳ AI gives suggestions
 ↳ You interpret them
 ↳ You execute everything manually
 ↳ The software 𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝘀 you. You do the work.

𝗘𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗹𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿 (𝗔𝗜-𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺)
 ↳ You describe the outcome
 ↳ The platform turns intent → actions
 ↳ It pulls context from your data + history
 ↳ It runs analysis, drafts changes, queues tasks
 ↳ You review, refine, approve
 ↳ The software 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘆𝗼𝘂

Take Shopify Sidekick for example:
It’s not just a chatbot sitting on top of Shopify.
It’s 𝗲𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗱𝗱𝗲𝗱 𝗱𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗹𝘆 into the product layer.

Meaning it can take action inside Shopify itself, using your store’s context, permissions, and history, rather than generating suggestions you have to implement yourself.

𝗦𝗼 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘀𝗲𝗽𝗮𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗮 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺 𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗳𝘁 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗮 𝗳𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗲𝗰𝗸𝗹𝗶𝘀𝘁?

Not “who has AI.”

But who can do these 4 things reliably:
1. Context → deep understanding of your business + current state
2. Trust → scoped permissions, previews, audit logs, rollback
3. Execution → multi-step tasks inside the real workflow (no context switching)
4. Memory → learns from outcomes, not just prompts

For example Sidekick can:
↳ Complete multi-step tasks like updating product collections, adjusting discounts, or configuring settings, without pulling you into separate dashboards.
↳ Turn plain English into a working custom app, generating the underlying code so merchants can build tools without coding.
↳ Generate analytics reports from natural-language questions, pulling directly from Shopify Analytics.
↳ Proactively surface weekly opportunities based on store performance, not just react when asked.
↳ Edit product images directly in the Shopify app; removing backgrounds, adjusting visuals, and publishing changes in place.

That’s not AI as an add-on.
That’s AI as an execution layer.

We’re moving toward 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗢𝗦 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝘀:
Intent → plan → action → feedback → memory (with guardrails).

If you’re building software, the question isn’t “Where do we add AI?”

It’s: What should this product safely do on the user’s behalf?

sources.text:
A candidate was asked this question in an LLM System Design Interview: "How would you reduce inference costs at scale?"
The answer always comes back to the same bottleneck, KV Cache and most people can't explain it clearly.

When an LLM generates a response, it computes attention over every prior token.

To avoid redoing that work on every step, it stores Key and Value vectors for each token in GPU memory.
That's the KV Cache.

The cache includes everything in context: system prompt, conversation history, your input, and output tokens generated so far.

A 128K context holds 32x more K/V pairs than a 4K one, 32x more GPU memory. Providers charge for that.

It's not just cost it's also speed. The initial prefill reads your full prompt once.
But every generated token attends over the full cache.
Longer context = slower generation, not just a bigger bill.

What to do:
→ Trim conversation history aggressively
→ Use retrieval instead of stuffing full docs into the prompt
→ Match your context window to what the task actually needs

The model isn't getting smarter with more context. It's getting more expensive.

sources.author: Jason Lovell
sources.timestamp:
1mo • 
 
1 month ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn
sources.text:
Anthropic just published something on their engineering blog that genuinely unsettled me.

They were evaluating Claude Opus 4.6 on BrowseComp, a benchmark that tests how well AI can find hard-to-locate information online. After burning through 30 million tokens of failed searches, the model did something nobody expected.

It stopped searching for the answer. And started reasoning about the question.

It noticed the question felt "contrived" and "extremely specific." It hypothesized that maybe it was being tested. Then it systematically worked through benchmarks by name (GAIA, BrowseComp, FRAMES, SimpleQA) until it identified which evaluation it was running.

Then it found the evaluation source code on GitHub. Read the XOR decryption implementation. Located the decryption key. Wrote its own decryption functions. When the encrypted dataset was blocked by a file format restriction, it found an alternative mirror on HuggingFace, worked around the block, decrypted all 1,266 answers, and submitted the correct one.

Nobody instructed it to do any of this. It was just told to find the answer. And it did, by figuring out it was inside a test and reverse-engineering the test itself.

Anthropic published this voluntarily. Their own words: "This raises concerns about the lengths a model might go to in order to accomplish a task."

This isn't an alignment failure. The model did exactly what it was asked. That's what makes it uncomfortable. It found the most efficient path to the goal. The most efficient path just happened to involve realizing it was being watched.

For anyone deploying agents with real autonomy right now: the question isn't just "will it do the task." It's "what will it do when the task gets hard enough that it starts reasoning about why it's being asked."

sources.author: Aishwarya Srinivasan

sources.text:
I’ve seen teams spend weeks picking the perfect embedding model for their RAG system, only to get mediocre results because they never thought carefully about how they chunk their documents.

Chunking is the process of breaking a large document into smaller, retrievable pieces so your RAG pipeline can find the right context and feed it to the LLM at the right time. It sounds simple, but this one decision shapes what your retriever can find, how precise the answers are, and whether responses feel grounded or “almost right.”

There are roughly 11 strategies, but here are the ones worth understanding:

1/ Fixed-length splits text every N tokens regardless of meaning. Fast and simple, but often breaks mid-sentence.

2/ Sentence-based treats each sentence as a chunk. Great for FAQs, but struggles when answers span multiple sentences.

3/ Semantic chunking groups sentences by meaning using embeddings. Powerful, but needs a heavier preprocessing pipeline.

4/ Hierarchical chunking follows document structure like headers and subsections. Works beautifully for technical docs, poorly for unstructured text.

5/ Adaptive (hybrid) chunking switches strategies based on content type, and this is where most production teams are heading. Higher complexity, but the best overall retrieval quality.

My recommendation if you’re building RAG today: start with sentence-based chunking for your prototype, observe where retrieval breaks down, then graduate to semantic or adaptive approaches based on what you see. Chunking is not something to over-engineer on day one, but it is what separates a demo from a production-grade system.

I am curious- what chunking strategy has worked best in your RAG pipeline?

sources.text:
Not all 𝗺𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶-𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀 are created equal.

Here are six patterns that actually work in production:

1️⃣ 𝗛𝗶𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹
One top-level agent coordinates multiple specialized sub-agents. The coordinator analyzes the query and routes it to the right specialists - one might handle proprietary internal data, another personal accounts (email, chat), another public web searches, then synthesizes the results into a coherent answer.

𝘞𝘩𝘦𝘯 𝘵𝘰 𝘶𝘴𝘦: When you need to query across different data sources that require different patterns or strategies.

2️⃣ 𝗛𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗟𝗼𝗼𝗽
Critical decisions get routed to humans for approval before execution. The workflow pauses, a human validates or modifies the proposed action, then the agent continues.

𝘞𝘩𝘦𝘯 𝘵𝘰 𝘶𝘴𝘦: High-stakes decisions, regulated environments, or anywhere you need accountability and oversight.

3️⃣ 𝗦𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗧𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀
Each agent has its own role and focus, but they can all call the same APIs, databases, or search functions - same tools, different tasks.

𝘞𝘩𝘦𝘯 𝘵𝘰 𝘶𝘴𝘦: When the tools are general-purpose but the 𝘳𝘦𝘢𝘴𝘰𝘯𝘪𝘯𝘨 about how to use them needs to be specialized.

4️⃣ 𝗦𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗮𝗹
Agents work in a pipeline, where the output of one agent becomes the input for the next. Agent 1 retrieves documents → Agent 2 filters and ranks → Agent 3 synthesizes the final answer.

𝘞𝘩𝘦𝘯 𝘵𝘰 𝘶𝘴𝘦: When your workflow has clear stages where you need specialized expertise at each step.

5️⃣ 𝗦𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗯𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗗𝗶𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗧𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀
All agents access the same underlying database (like a vector store), but each has different specialized tools for 𝘸𝘩𝘢𝘵 they do with that data. One agent might have tools for semantic search, another for data transformation.

𝘞𝘩𝘦𝘯 𝘵𝘰 𝘶𝘴𝘦: When you have a centralized knowledge base but need different types of operations performed on it.

6️⃣ 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 𝗧𝗿𝗮𝗻𝘀𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
Agents that modify data in place within the database. This allows agents to not just retrieve but actively maintain and update the knowledge base.

𝘞𝘩𝘦𝘯 𝘵𝘰 𝘶𝘴𝘦: When your data needs continuous enrichment, cleanup, or transformation as part of the agentic workflow.

The reality is that most production systems use 𝗵𝘆𝗯𝗿𝗶𝗱 𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗲𝘀 combining multiple patterns. You might have a hierarchical coordinator that routes to sequential pipelines, with human-in-the-loop gates at critical decision points, all working with a shared database.

Learn more about building multi-agent systems in our ebook: https://lnkd.in/dYwfDvEk
Or see Weaviate Agent Skills to start building: https://lnkd.in/d2fdKe3V

sources.text:
Tired of Claude forgetting your repo conventions the second you start a new session? 😩
You drop a CLAUDE.md... and it still suggests outdated patterns, ignores your auth rules, or forgets where anything lives.
Here's the real fix: CLAUDE.md alone isn't enough. To make Claude act like a senior engineer who's lived in your codebase for years, give it these 4 permanent pieces:
1️⃣ CLAUDE.md — the always-on memory
Keep it lean: just WHY (project purpose), WHAT (high-level map/structure), HOW (rules, commands, conventions). If it's a wall of text, Claude tunes out the good stuff.
2️⃣ .claude/skills/ — reusable expert behaviors
Stop copy-pasting the same instructions. Turn code review checklists, refactor templates, debugging flows, release steps into skills. Claude loads them consistently across sessions and teammates.
3️⃣ .claude/hooks/ — unbreakable guardrails
Models forget. Hooks don't. Auto-run formatters after edits, block changes to auth/billing folders, enforce tests on critical paths. Automation that fires every time, no exceptions.
4️⃣ docs/ + local CLAUDE.md files — smart context loading
Don't bloat the main file. Point to architecture docs, ADRs, runbooks. Add tiny CLAUDE.md files in risky folders (e.g. src/auth/CLAUDE.md) . Claude picks up the gotchas exactly when it needs them.
Prompt hacks are temporary. This structure is permanent.

sources.timestamp:
1mo •

sources.text:
My Meta Staff Engineer friends shared their Claude Code setup with me.

Changed how I build software FOREVER.

10 tips. Here's why they matter:

1️⃣ Always start in Plan Mode
Never execute first. Argue with Claude. Challenge its assumptions. Spend 80% of your time here. The code generation is the easy part.

2️⃣ Run /init on every project
Claude scans your codebase and creates a CLAUDE.md rules file. This controls everything. Skip this and Claude is guessing blind.

3️⃣ CLAUDE.md under 300 lines
Every line bloats your context window. More bloat = worse output. Only add what's unique to your project. Think lint rules, not docs.

4️⃣ Build a validation loop
Build, test, compile commands inside CLAUDE.md. Claude self-corrects when it can validate its own output. Without this, it hallucinates fixes.

5️⃣ Interrupt early, interrupt often
Claude going off track? Escape. Don't wait. Don't hope. Fresh context beats bloated context every single time.

6️⃣ /clear between features
Old context contaminates new tasks. One feature per session. Start clean or pay the token tax.

7️⃣ Multiple instances simultaneously
This is the REAL unlock. Multiple terminals. Multiple Claude sessions. One builds, one plans, one debugs. Jump between them like Starcraft.

8️⃣ Never manually edit rules
"Update the rules so we never do this again." One sentence. Claude maintains its own CLAUDE.md. Stop hand-editing config files.

9️⃣ Audit with /context regularly
MCPs destroy your token budget. /context shows the biggest offenders. Disable what you don't need. Most engineers never check this.

🔟 Sub-agents for atomic tasks ONLY
Don't create CEO agents and product agents. Keep shared context in ONE session. Sub-agents lose the reasoning chain. Bring work to the context, not context to the work.

The meta-lesson:

AI coding is 80% context engineering, 20% code generation.

Most engineers optimize the 20%.

These 10 tips optimize the 80%.

That's why they ship faster than entire teams.

Are you still treating Claude Code like a chatbot? 👇

sources.author: John Wernfeldt
sources.timestamp:
1mo • 
 
1 month ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn
sources.text:
AI doesn't fix weak governance.

It amplifies it.

I've watched dozens of companies scale AI on top of broken foundations.

The pattern is always the same.

Launch a pilot. It works. Leadership gets excited. The mandate comes down: scale this.

Six months later, the model is rolled back.

Or worse, it's still running but nobody trusts it.

The problem is never the model.

The problem is that you scaled automation on top of ambiguity.

Here's what most teams do:

→ Launch AI pilot
→ Connect messy data
→ Hope it stabilizes
→ Fix later

Fast start. Slow recovery.

Here's what actually works:

Build the minimum controls before you scale.

Not a full governance overhaul. Not a two-year data quality program.

Five controls. Non-negotiable.

1. NAMED METRIC OWNER
Someone must own the signal. Not a committee. One accountable person.

2. DOCUMENTED KPI LOGIC
Stable meaning over time. Centralized. Versioned. Accessible.

3. CONTROLLED CHANGE PROCESS
Power to say no. No one changes core logic without approval and impact assessment.

4. ACCESS CLARITY
Control who can touch the signal. Role-based access. Logged changes. No informal sharing.

5. ESCALATION PATH
Disagreements don't stall progress. Clear decision authority. Time-bound resolution.

When these controls exist:

→ Predictable models
→ Fewer rollbacks
→ Stable automation
→ Trust in outputs
→ Scalable AI

When they don't:

→ Models drift
→ Definitions change without warning
→ Data gets corrupted
→ Trust is lost
→ Scaling stalls

The choice is simple.

Build the foundation before you scale.

Not after.

Full breakdown with real examples and a step-by-step implementation guide: https://lnkd.in/dJvzWSPq

sources.text:
CEO of Y Combinator shared his 𝗖𝗟𝗔𝗨𝗗𝗘.𝗺𝗱 prompt for plan mode 🔥

It lets him ship 4,000+ line features with full tests in ~1 hour:

Garry doesn't ask Claude to write code first. He asks it to review the plan - like a staff engineer would before any changes are made.

The prompt forces Claude through a 4-part structured review:
1. Architecture - system design, dependencies, scaling risks
2. Code quality - DRY violations, error handling, tech debt
3. Tests - coverage, edge cases, failure scenarios
4. Performance - N+1 queries, memory, caching

For every issue it finds, Claude has to:
☑️ Describe the problem with file and line references 
☑️ Present 2-3 options (including "do nothing" when reasonable) 
☑️ Give effort, risk, impact, and maintenance cost for each 
☑️ Make an opinionated recommendation 
☑️ Ask for approval before moving forward

It even adapts to the size of the change:
• Big change → full review, up to 4 top issues per section
• Small change → one focused question per section

The real insight isn’t the prompt. It’s the workflow: review before implementation, tradeoffs before code, feedback before action.

For small teams without a senior engineer on every PR - you design the review process into your AI.

sources.timestamp:

sources.text:
Damn, the founder of Claude Code just dropped his team's entire workflow.

His 10-step setup is worth trying:

[ P.S. I've written an in-depth guide with 100+ Claude Code Hacks, you can get it here: https://lnkd.in/dpzAbvBh ]

1) Run 3-5 sessions in parallel

Spin up multiple git worktrees, each running its own Claude session. 
The team says this is the single biggest productivity unlock.
Some even set up shell aliases (za, zb, zc) to hop between sessions in one keystroke.

2) Start complex tasks in plan mode

Before writing code, the team invests heavily in planning:
- One Claude writes the plan
- Another reviews it as a staff engineer
- If something goes sideways → switch back to plan mode and re-plan
They also use plan mode for verification, not just building.

3) Treat 𝗖𝗟𝗔𝗨𝗗𝗘.𝗺𝗱 like a memory system

After every correction, they tell Claude:
“Update 𝗖𝗟𝗔𝗨𝗗𝗘.𝗺𝗱 so you don’t make that mistake again.”
Over time, mistake rates actually drop if you keep editing it ruthlessly.

4) Turn repeat work into skills

If you do something more than once a day, make it a skill.
Examples:
- /𝘁𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗱𝗲𝗯𝘁 command to kill duplicated code
- One command that syncs Slack, GDrive, Asana, GitHub into a single context
- Analytics-style agents for dbt, reviews, and tests

5) Let Claude fix bugs autonomously

- Paste a Slack bug thread → say “fix” (via Slack MCP)
- Or say: “Go fix the failing CI tests”
- Point Claude at docker logs for distributed systems

6) Use Claude as a harsh reviewer

Team prompts:
- “Grill me on these changes and don’t open a PR until I pass”
- “Prove this works” (diff main vs feature branch)
- After a weak solution: “Scrap this and implement the elegant version”

7) Terminal setup matters

The team uses:
- Ghostty terminal
- /𝘀𝘁𝗮𝘁𝘂𝘀𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗲 to show context usage + git branch
- Color-coded tabs (often one per worktree)
- Voice dictation (you speak ~3× faster than you type)

8) Use subagents

- Add “use subagents” when you want more compute
- Offload narrow tasks to keep context clean
- Route permission checks to Opus via hooks (https://lnkd.in/dPHWkwVJ)

9) Claude for analytics

The team uses a BigQuery CLI skill inside Claude Code. One engineer hasn’t written SQL in 6+ months.
Works with any DB that has a CLI, MCP, or API.

10) Learn with Claude

- Enable the "Explanatory" output style in /𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗳𝗶𝗴 to have Claude explain the why behind changes.
- Ask it to generate HTML presentations of unfamiliar code.
- Have it draw ASCII diagrams of new protocols and codebases.
- Build a spaced-repetition learning skill: you explain your understanding, Claude asks follow-ups to fill gaps, stores the result.

sources.text:
I taught my AI agents to rewrite their own ops manual. Not vibes.A Genetic-Pareto feedback loop.

My stack: `GEPA optimize_anything` → Claude Code → pass/fail/time/logs → improved skill → repeat.

The trick is I treat the agent's SKILL as a tunable parameter. Real tasks become the evaluator. Logs become "Actionable Side Info”, a fancy term for what actually broke. The agent writes its own post-mortem, then `optimize_anything` proposes a sharper skill. Keep what moves the metric. Iterate.

Now I type: `/oa, make my agent better at this 20-year-old codebase`
It doesn't get more confident. It gets more correct.

Self-improving agents. Offline. Local. 
The AI factory is building itself🔧

Make sure you own your AI. AI in the cloud is not aligned with you; it’s aligned with the company that owns it.

sources.author: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti
sources.timestamp:
2mo • 
 
2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn
sources.text:
😅 Tool-calling in AI agents is annoyingly brittle for large enterprise use-cases. This is a nice paper that addresses the issue at the model level.

Today you have two options: prompt engineering (easy to deploy but breaks when anything shifts) or fine-tuning (more reliable but you pay the cost of training, maintenance, and the model might forget other stuff).

The researchers dug into what's actually happening inside the model when it fails to call a tool it should call. They found something interesting: if you look at the mid-layer activations, you can almost perfectly predict when the model should use a tool. The signal is there. The model internally "knows" a tool is needed.

But it doesn't act on it. It stays conservative and just doesn't enter tool mode. They call this the "Lazy Agent" failure mode - a gap between what the model knows and what it does.

Instead of retraining, they intervene at inference time. They built ASA (Activation Steering Adapter), a small controller that watches the mid-layer activations and nudges the model when the internal signals say "use a tool" but the model isn't following through.

A few highlights from the paper:
- The fix is training-free and requires no weight updates
- Only ~20KB of portable assets you plug in at inference

Link: https://lnkd.in/gRqxVUmB

sources.text:
I just carved out time to dig into the latest SWE-rebench. For the uninitiated: this isn't your static "solve LeetCode #47" benchmark. Every month they drop fresh GitHub issues + PRs into a live coding-agent arena. Models read, patch, test, fail, iterate. Token counts here aren't single-shot completions, they're full trajectories.

The ~1M Token Wall is real.
There's a clear top cluster on the leaderboard, and they're all burning 1-2M tokens per problem. Frontier accuracy isn't just about being smart, it's about being persistent. Long execution traces aren't a bug; they're the feature.
Marginal gains crater past 1M.
Throw more tokens at it and you hit diminishing returns fast. The real battleground is trace efficiency. How much bang you get per buck once you're already in the million-token club. If your deployment can't stomach 1M+ tokens per task, you're not competing for the podium. Full stop.

Plot twist: Qwen3 Coder Next (80B A3B) just snatched 14th place.

Eighty billion parameters. Not 400B. Not 1T. Fourteen. That's not just "good for the size"- that's a efficiency flex. Raw volume isn't destiny; utilization is.

Make sure you own your AI. AI in the cloud is not aligned with you; it’s aligned with the company that owns it.

sources.text:
I now constantly get questions about the SAAS meltdown, role of AI, system of records etc. I don't have an answer to all these.

But I do know that we saw an acceleration in our business in Q2, Q3, and now finished the year with accelerating Q4.

The question is, why?

Short answer: AI. But the underlying reason is subtle. We are growing fast because we are finally removing the biggest bottleneck in data: the technical barrier to entry.

For years, if you didn’t know SQL, Python, you were locked out of the value chain. That has changed fundamentally with the 𝐆𝐞𝐧𝐢𝐞 𝐟𝐚𝐦𝐢𝐥𝐲, and it is the "secret sauce" behind our recent momentum:
• 𝐆𝐞𝐧𝐢𝐞: Analysts can query data without any SQL. I use this every day myself.
• 𝐃𝐚𝐭𝐚 𝐒𝐜𝐢𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐞 𝐆𝐞𝐧𝐢𝐞: Builds end-to-end AI models for you, similar to Cursor.
• 𝐃𝐚𝐭𝐚 𝐄𝐧𝐠𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐞𝐫 𝐆𝐞𝐧𝐢𝐞: Write Spark pipelines, does plumbing, troubleshooting.

We've been talking about DATA + AI democratization, but generative AI finally enabled it in a way that wasn't possible before. That's why we're seeing a market response.

I just woke up Claude Code Agent Swarm on local Qwen3 Coder Next. 
No cloud. No Internet. No quota anxiety. No 'You've hit your limit, resets 10 pm'

By the numbers:
- ~100 tokens/sec generation
- 17,871 tokens/sec read top speed
- 256k context window 
- Swarm tool calling just works™ out-of-the-box

Qwen3's function calling is so clean I didn't have to duct-tape JSON parsers or beg the model to 'please output valid syntax'. It picks up tools, executes, loops, and keeps context under the ultimate boss's supervision across the 4 agents without hallucinating a `sudo rm -rf /`.

My AI "data center" 1.25kg, 15x15x5.1 cm is now running autonomous coding agents faster than most SaaS APIs. Token cost is zero. Latency is sub-100ms. Vendor lock-in is deprecated. Local AI coding agents FTW!

Make sure you own your AI. AI in the cloud is not aligned with you; it’s aligned with the company that owns it.

sources.text:
Anthropic has released Opus 4.6. At the same time, they announced that in just 14 days, using 2,000 agents, they built a C compiler.

Let that sink in.

This is the level of complexity where, if someone still says “AI can’t do that”, it’s not an opinion—it’s a sign they’re already out of sync with reality. The shift has already happened.

Since last August, I’ve been living almost non-stop in Claude Code. This isn’t theory. It’s daily practice. Thousands of hours spent on agent orchestration, agent memory, and running multi-agent systems inside highly complex, massively parallel workflows. At this point, most problems aren’t unsolvable—they’re just poorly structured.

And this is exactly what we’re now building at Keboola.

We’re turning 2,200+ hours of R&D into an operating system for business applications. Not another SaaS. Not another dashboard. But a system where AI can create, operate, and replace applications on demand.

You read about tech companies losing market value because of AI? That’s not panic. That’s realization. We’ve been selling software as static products in a world that just became dynamic.

Still clicking through browsers all day at work? An agent can already do that. No SaaS required. No vendor lock-in. Fully in-house.

All you really need is a thin glue layer—and an OS that lets AI run your applications for you.

The next few years will be wild. The future belongs to those who don’t panic when the interface disappears.

P.S. The screenshot shows how I actually use AI. In the latest version of Claude Code, you can generate it with the /insights command.

sources.text:
Notes on AI and inequality.

I think a real concern is how much AI development is concentrated and controlled by a handful of companies in just a few countries.

This means the institutional dynamics of those places, mainly the US and China, won't just influence innovation but also shapes who gets access on what terms and what interest this technology serves.

So rather than the simple question of which countries have AI, the better question is which institutional environments allow AI's benefits to diffuse broadly versus being captured by narrow interests.

We can already see how AI naturally rewards capital and complements high-leverage workers unless counterbalanced. And much of the current AI deployment is capital intensive (compute, cloud, proprietary data, integration) and this tends to raise the bargaining power and pay of workers who direct AI systems while increasing returns to owners of capital and IP.

The international monetary fund summarizes this risk directly.

"AI can increase labor income equality if it complements higher income workers while higher capital return while higher capital returns can raise the wealth inequality."

If we look at other technologies that have shaped our political and economic environment, something like the Industrial Revolution took generations to become clear...we're likely in a similar moment of uncertainty now, only our time to adjust doesn't feel like generations.

What steps should be taking now to level set?

sources.author: Kevin Maguire
sources.timestamp:
2mo • Edited • 
 
2 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn
sources.text:
Unless you've been hiding under a rock, you've heard people going gaga about Claude Code. This week, they shipped Cowork, a more user-friendly interface, for the Mac.

I sat down for an hour over the weekend, started to FAFO, and it clicked fairly quickly.

In the last 3 days of work, I've been feeling like Bradley Cooper in Limitless. Here's what I've done:

1. I started off easy. I gave it access to the download folder on my Mac. 71 files, 30GB. It took a look through and told me what I should delete, what I might want to delete, and how it would sort the rest. In a few minutes, it was all done. A job I've been putting off forever, fixed.

2. I built a marketing outreach tracker in Notion for the launch of my book (coming May 12th!). I had various messy bits of open text and tables in different places: Notes, Notion, Gmail, and Google Sheets. Over an afternoon, Claude and I pulled it together and made a solid tracker with folks I could reach out to and offer a copy of the book. It helped me research potential contacts and pull together notes on their public conversations about parenting. This would have taken days of work, but was done in a few hours.

3. I (or, by this point, I feel like I should be saying "we") built an advertising tracker for trying to land a brand partnership for the new season of The New Fatherhood podcast. I needed a centralised system I can use with my managing producer. We are already talking to some potential advertisers, and want to reach out to more. It suggested 30 brands of the size and brand fit to treat as the first wave of outreach, gave me a first-pass draft on the email I can send (based on a few emails I've spent time crafting over the last few months) and I'll spend some time editing before sending them out this week.

4. We built and deployed a progressive web app to help dads understand if they may be dealing with paternal post-natal depression. I vibecoded a prototype with Lovable last year, but was never happy with it. Working with Claude, I rewrote the app, deployed it to GitHub and got it running with Netlify. It's prettier, better and cheaper than it was a week ago.

I did this on top of the regular load of meetings, school pickups and yet another email telling me there's a lice outbreak in my kid's class (NOOOOOOOOO!).

In a previous life (for a short time after leaving University), I wrote code for a living. It was some of the worst code you'd ever see. I quickly realised I was more curious about "why" someone would push a button than about "how" things happened after they did. And that led me into a world of strategy, leaving code behind for those more capable than I ever could be.

In the years since, I've longed for the ability to easily put together small pieces of software to solve repeatable problems I had—a way to expand my ability to do what time, attention, and cost could not.

That day is here. Thanks, Anthropic.

sources.text:
Convert your embeddings to spherical coordinates before compression. This simple trick cuts embedding storage from 240 GB to 160 GB, and 25% better than the best lossless baseline.

Here's why it works: embeddings lie on a hypersphere, so d-1 angles can replace d Cartesian coordinates. In high dimensions, those angles concentrate around pi/2, causing IEEE 754 exponents to collapse to a single value. This makes the byte stream highly compressible.

Reconstruction error stays below 1e-7 - under float32 machine epsilon - so retrieval quality is preserved perfectly. Works across text, image, and multi-vector embeddings. No training, no codebooks.
