sources.title: zjkBMFhNj
directClaims: A large language model is fundamentally just two files: a parameters file and a runtime file that executes those parameters
Llama 2 70B model has 140 gigabytes of parameters stored as float16 numbers (2 bytes each)
Training Llama 2 70B requires roughly 10 terabytes of text, 6,000 GPUs, 12 days of compute, and costs about $2 million
Model training is lossy compression of internet text, similar to a zip file but with information loss
Large language models perform next-word prediction as their fundamental objective
Pre-training uses large quantities of internet text (potentially low quality), while fine-tuning uses smaller quantities of high-quality curated Q&A data
Fine-tuning to create an assistant model typically uses 100,000 high-quality documents created by human labelers
Scaling laws show that model performance is a predictable function of two variables: number of parameters (N) and amount of training text (D)
Current state-of-the-art models (GPT-4, Claude, Bard) require training budgets 10x or more larger than Llama 2 70B
Proprietary models (GPT, Claude) currently outperform open-source models but are only accessible via web interfaces
Large language models lack System 2 thinking and cannot currently perform extended reasoning or tree-of-thought search
AlphaGo surpassed human players through self-play rather than imitation learning alone
Tool use including web browsing, code execution, image generation, and mathematical calculation increasingly enable LLM capability
Multimodality (text, image, audio, video) is a major axis of improvement for large language models
inferredBeliefs: The author believes LLMs should be understood as operating system kernels orchestrating resources rather than simple chatbots
The field will increasingly focus on extending model reasoning through System 2 capabilities and longer inference-time computation
Self-improvement beyond human performance is a key frontier, though currently limited by lack of clear reward functions in open-ended domains
The LLM ecosystem will follow patterns similar to historical operating systems: proprietary systems competing with open-source alternatives
Tool use and multimodality are essential for practical capability, not peripheral features
Current understanding of how neural network parameters collaborate is fundamentally limited despite knowing the architecture
Scaling (larger models, more data) will continue to drive capability improvements without necessarily requiring algorithmic breakthroughs
Fine-tuning and customization will become increasingly important for domain-specific applications
workflowPatterns: Training pipeline: collect internet text → procure GPU cluster → train model → obtain parameters file and runtime code
Fine-tuning pipeline: write labeling instructions → hire labelers → create Q&A examples → fine-tune base model → evaluate → deploy → collect failures → fix failures iteratively
Multi-stage improvement: Stage 1 (pre-training on internet text) → Stage 2 (fine-tuning on Q&A data) → Stage 3 (optional: training with comparison labels via RLHF)

Tool-use workflow: LLM identifies need for tool → emits special tokens → external tool executes (search, calculator, code interpreter, image generation) → results fed back to LLM → LLM generates response
Customization through retrieval: upload custom documents → LLM references chunks from documents via retrieval augmented generation → LLM generates contextualized responses
Iterative problem-solving: ask question → LLM uses tools to research → organize findings → generate visualization → extrapolate results → generate images based on analysis
sources.summary: Karpathy provides a comprehensive introduction to large language models, explaining that they are fundamentally neural networks trained to predict the next word in a sequence, composed of just two files (parameters and runtime code). He details the two-stage training process (pre-training on internet text and fine-tuning on curated Q&A data), discusses scaling laws that predictably improve performance with more parameters and data, and demonstrates concrete capabilities including tool use, multimodality, and extended problem-solving. He concludes by framing LLMs as emerging operating system kernels coordinating computational resources and tools, identifying key future research directions including System 2 reasoning, self-improvement mechanisms, and task-specific customization.

sources.title: zduSFxRajkE
directClaims: Tokenization is the process for translating strings or text into sequences of tokens and vice versa
GPT-2 uses a vocabulary of 50,257 possible tokens with a context size of 1,024 tokens
The Llama 2 paper mentions 'tokens' 63 times and states they trained on two trillion tokens of data
Unicode defines roughly 150,000 characters across 161 scripts as of Unicode 15.1 in September 2023
UTF-8 encoding takes every Unicode code point and translates it to a byte stream between one to four bytes, making it variable-length encoding
The Byte Pair Encoding algorithm iteratively finds the most frequently occurring pair of tokens and replaces it with a single new token
GPT-4 tokenizer has approximately 100,000 tokens, roughly double that of GPT-2's 50,000
GPT-4 tokenizer improved handling of whitespace for Python by grouping multiple spaces into single tokens
Non-English languages typically require more tokens than English for the same semantic content due to tokenizer training biases
A tokenizer is a completely separate preprocessing stage from the language model with its own training set
Strings in Python are immutable sequences of Unicode code points
Many issues in large language models trace back to tokenization, including spelling tasks, arithmetic, and non-English language performance
inferredBeliefs: Tokenization is complex and often overlooked despite being fundamental to LLM behavior
Understanding tokenization details is critical for debugging and optimizing LLM performance
The design choices in tokenizer architecture have significant downstream effects on model capabilities
There is room for innovation in tokenization approaches beyond Byte Pair Encoding
Tokenizer training data composition should be deliberately chosen based on target use cases and languages
UTF-8 is the correct encoding standard for modern systems despite some inefficiencies
Vocabulary size in tokenizers represents a tradeoff between sequence length and embedding table size
workflowPatterns: Encode raw text string to UTF-8 bytes to get initial token sequence
Use get_stats() function to count frequency of all consecutive token pairs in sequence
Identify the most frequently occurring pair in the token sequence
Mint a new token ID for that pair and add to vocabulary
Replace all occurrences of that pair with the new token ID throughout the sequence
Record the merge operation (child tokens → new token ID) in a merges dictionary
Repeat merging process iteratively until desired vocabulary size is reached
Build vocabulary mapping from token ID to byte representation using merges dictionary
For decoding: iterate through token IDs, look up byte representations, concatenate bytes, decode UTF-8 to string
For encoding: convert string to UTF-8 bytes, then iteratively apply merges in order until no more merges possible
Store tokenizer separately from language model as a preprocessing stage before training
sources.summary: Andrej Karpathy provides a comprehensive deep dive into tokenization in large language models, explaining why it matters, how it works at a fundamental level, and walking through a complete implementation of Byte Pair Encoding from scratch. He demonstrates how tokenization directly impacts LLM capabilities across languages, code, arithmetic, and other domains through interactive examples and live code walkthroughs.

sources.title: t3YJ5hKiMQ0
directClaims: We are implementing makemore, a character-level language model
The architecture takes multiple previous characters as input to predict the next character
We started with 3 characters of context and expanded to 8 characters
WaveNet is a paper published by DeepMind in 2016 that predicts audio sequences using similar autoregressive modeling
The dataset contains 182,000 examples of three characters predicting a fourth character
We developed layer modules including Linear, BatchNorm1d, and Tanh layers to mimic PyTorch APIs
BatchNorm has running mean and variance trained via exponential moving average during forward pass
BatchNorm behaves differently during training and evaluation, requiring careful state management
We implemented a Sequential container to organize layers into lists
Initial validation loss was 2.10, improved to 2.02 by increasing context length from 3 to 8
We implemented hierarchical progressive fusion of information rather than crushing all characters into a single layer
The hierarchical architecture uses consecutive flattening where pairs of characters are progressively fused through deeper layers
BatchNorm1d was incorrectly averaging only over batch dimension instead of batch and sequence dimensions for 3D inputs
The fix involved reducing over dimensions (0,1) instead of just (0) for 3D tensor inputs
After fixing BatchNorm, validation loss improved from 2.029 to 2.022
With increased embedding size (24) and hidden units, we achieved validation loss of 1.993 with 76,000 parameters
Convolutions are primarily used for efficiency in WaveNet implementations, not for changing the model itself
Convolutional approaches allow variable reuse and efficient sliding of filters over input sequences
PyTorch's BatchNorm1d expects channels in the middle dimension (N x C x L) while our implementation expects channels in the last dimension
inferredBeliefs: Building neural networks from first principles using basic operations deepens understanding of deep learning
Careful implementation of standardization layers is critical because they introduce state and mode-dependent behavior
Hierarchical architectures that progressively fuse information are more efficient than immediate full concatenation
Explicit dimension handling is preferable to implicit dimension resolution for catching errors
Model capacity should be measured by parameter count while architecture determines efficiency of parameter usage
Experimentation at scale requires systematic logging and tracking of hyperparameters and results
Understanding implementation details of standard libraries like PyTorch is valuable for debugging and optimization
workflowPatterns: Read dataset and process into character-level examples
Create embedding table to convert character integers to vectors
Flatten consecutive embeddings using view operations
Pass through linear layers with batch normalization and activation functions
Monitor training and validation loss curves with smoothing
Sample from model by iteratively predicting next character given context
Inspect tensor shapes at each layer during debugging
Use break statements and temporary batch creation for debugging forward passes
Progressively increase model complexity and capacity through architecture changes
Set layers to eval mode before validation to disable training-specific behavior
sources.summary: Andrej implements a character-level language model (makemore) by progressively building from a simple multi-layer perceptron to a hierarchical WaveNet-inspired architecture that fuses information progressively rather than immediately concatenating all context. The video covers implementing core PyTorch modules from scratch, fixing subtle bugs in BatchNorm for 3D inputs, and improving validation loss from 2.10 to 1.99 through architectural changes and increased model capacity.

sources.title: q8SA3rM6ckI
directClaims: Back propagation is a leaky abstraction - you can shoot yourself in the foot if you don't understand its internals
Saturating activation functions cause gradients to die
Dead neurons are a known issue in neural networks
Exploding or vanishing gradients occur in recurrent neural networks
Writing backward passes by hand was standard practice about 10 years ago in deep learning
Today, writing your backward pass by hand manually is not recommended except for purposes of exercise
PyTorch's autograd engine calculates gradients automatically, removing the need for manual backward pass implementation
The max operation in softmax is used for numerical stability to prevent overflow
Changing the logit maximum does not change the softmax probabilities
Matrix multiplication backward pass can be derived from first principles by breaking down small examples
The derivative of loss with respect to logits in cross-entropy equals the negative mean of selected log probabilities
Dimensions must align correctly in matrix operations - there is only one way to make shapes work out
Broadcasting in PyTorch automatically replicates tensors to align dimensions
inferredBeliefs: Deep understanding of backpropagation internals is essential for effective neural network debugging
Pedagogical value comes from implementing concepts from scratch before using automatic tools
Mathematical reasoning on paper using small examples clarifies complex tensor operations
Explicit implementation with intermediate tensors aids understanding over collapsed abstract operations
Historical context and evolution of deep learning practices provides valuable perspective
Dimensional analysis is a powerful tool for deriving correct formulas without memorization
Gradient checking against automatic differentiation is a crucial validation technique
workflowPatterns: Forward pass: compute intermediate tensors with retain_grad() for all values needed in backward
Initialize network with small random numbers instead of zeros to avoid masking gradient errors
Implement backward pass manually by computing d-variables in reverse topological order
Use gradient checking to compare manually computed gradients against PyTorch's autograd
Derive local derivatives for each operation mathematically
Apply chain rule by multiplying local derivatives with upstream gradients
Handle broadcasting by summing gradients across replicated dimensions with keepdim=True
Use dimensional analysis to determine correct matrix multiplication order
Validate intermediate results using comparison function before proceeding
sources.summary: Andrej Karpathy walks through implementing backpropagation manually at the tensor level instead of using PyTorch's autograd, demonstrating the complete forward and backward pass for a two-layer multilayer perceptron with batch normalization. He emphasizes that understanding backpropagation internals is crucial for debugging neural networks and uses small mathematical examples to derive correct gradient formulas, showing that dimensional analysis alone can guide the correct implementation without memorizing formulas.

sources.title: l8pRSuU81PU
directClaims: GPT-2 was released by OpenAI in 2019 with a blog post, paper, and GitHub code
The 124 million parameter model is the smallest in the GPT-2 miniseries, ranging from 124M to 1.5B parameters
GPT-2 124M has 12 layers in the Transformer with 768 channels/dimensions
GPT-2 uses a decoder-only Transformer architecture, not an encoder-decoder like the original Transformer
GPT-2 applies layer normalization before attention and feed-forward (pre-normalization), unlike the original Transformer which applies it after
An additional layer normalization was added before the final classifier in GPT-2
GPT-2 uses the approximate GELU nonlinearity (10h) rather than the exact version
GPT-2 uses multi-headed attention with queries, keys, and values
The GPT-2 vocabulary size is 50,257 tokens
GPT-2 has a maximum sequence length of 1,024 tokens
The positional embeddings in GPT-2 are learned parameters, not fixed sinusoids like in the original Transformer
You can reproduce the GPT-2 124M model in roughly one hour on cloud compute for about $10
OpenAI released the weights for GPT-2 publicly
The original GPT-2 code was written in TensorFlow
Attention is a communication/aggregation operation where tokens exchange information
MLP operates individually on each token with no information exchange between tokens
The Transformer is a repeated application of map and reduce operations
Hugging Face Transformers library converted GPT-2 weights from TensorFlow to PyTorch
Top-K sampling with K=50 is used in the default Hugging Face pipeline for generation
inferredBeliefs: Prefers PyTorch over TensorFlow for modern deep learning work
Values interpretability and understanding of model internals over using black-box implementations
Believes in building models from scratch to gain full understanding rather than relying on library abstractions
Sees historical quirks in ML (like approximate GELU) as interesting artifacts worth understanding
Thinks clean residual pathways are important for optimization and gradient flow
Believes GPU acceleration is essential for practical neural network training
Prefers minimal, readable implementations over feature-complete library code
Values reproducibility and ability to verify results against official releases
Thinks step-by-step visualization of learned parameters (like positional embeddings) is scientifically interesting
Believes mechanistic interpretability of neural networks is worth exploring
mentionedTools: Jupyter Notebook extension for VS Code
workflowPatterns: Load pretrained weights from Hugging Face to verify model architecture correctness
Generate text from loaded model to validate implementation
Implement model from scratch to match pretrained architecture
Load state dict from Hugging Face and port weights to custom implementation
Handle weight transposition when converting between TensorFlow and PyTorch formats
Put model in eval mode and move to GPU before generation
Encode text prefix using tokenizer to get initial token indices
Generate tokens autoregressively by sampling from probability distribution
Use top-K sampling to keep generation on track
Load raw text dataset and tokenize it
Create batches by reshaping 1D token sequences into 2D tensors (batch × time)
Offset input and target tensors to align labels with predictions
Calculate loss between model logits and ground truth labels
Perform backward pass and optimization step
sources.summary: This video walks through reproducing the GPT-2 124 million parameter model from scratch. Karpathy loads the official OpenAI weights using Hugging Face Transformers, implements a custom PyTorch GPT-2 class to match the architecture, and generates text to verify correctness before moving to training from random initialization on the tiny Shakespeare dataset.

sources.title: kCc8FmEb1nY
directClaims: ChatGPT is a system that allows you to interact with an AI and give it text-based tasks
ChatGPT is a probabilistic system that can generate multiple different outputs for the same prompt
ChatGPT is a language model that models the sequence of words, characters, or tokens
The Transformer architecture comes from the 2017 paper 'Attention is All You Need'
GPT stands for 'Generatively Pre-trained Transformer'
The Transformer architecture from 2017 took over the rest of AI in the 5 years after its publication
ChatGPT is trained on a good chunk of the internet with pre-training and fine-tuning stages
Tiny Shakespeare is a concatenation of all of Shakespeare's works in a single 1 megabyte file
The character-level language model will predict characters sequentially based on previous context
nanoGPT is a repository of two 300-line files: one defining the GPT model and one training it
The nanoGPT codebase reproduces the performance of GPT-2's smallest 124 million parameter model
Google uses SentencePiece as a subword tokenizer
OpenAI has a library called tiktoken that uses byte pair encoding tokenizer
Block size is the maximum context length for making predictions in a Transformer
A single chunk of nine characters contains eight individual training examples
The Transformer is trained on all context lengths from 1 to block size
Batch size determines how many independent sequences are processed per forward-backward pass
The Bigram language model predicts the next character based only on the identity of the current token
Cross entropy loss measures the quality of predictions against target values
The initial random model achieves a loss of approximately 4.17 for a uniform 65-character vocabulary
Self-attention allows tokens to communicate with their preceding context but not future tokens
A bag-of-words approach averages all preceding tokens to create context-aware representations
Lower triangular matrices can be used with matrix multiplication to efficiently implement causal attention

Understanding the fundamentals of neural networks is crucial for grasping how modern AI works

sources.summary: Andrej Karpathy builds a character-level language model from scratch using PyTorch, starting with a simple Bigram model and progressively introducing the Transformer architecture to understand how ChatGPT works. The video demonstrates tokenization, batching, loss computation, and text generation using the Tiny Shakespeare dataset, while introducing key concepts like self-attention and efficient matrix operations that form the foundation of modern language models.

sources.title: TCH
directClaims: The bigram model only takes one character of previous context to predict the next character
The problem with the bigram approach is that if you take more context, the table size grows exponentially with context length
Taking two characters as context gives 27×27=729 possibilities; taking three gives 20,000 possibilities
We implement a multi-layer perceptron model following the Bengio et al. 2003 paper
The paper proposes embedding 17,000 words into a 30-dimensional space
Word embeddings allow the model to generalize to unseen phrases by learning that similar words have nearby embeddings
We initialize embedding lookup table C randomly, then optimize it via backpropagation
The hidden layer size is a hyperparameter that can vary
The output layer has 17,000 neurons for a word-level model (or 27 for character-level)
PyTorch's view() operation efficiently reshapes tensors without copying memory by manipulating storage metadata
f.cross_entropy is more efficient than manually computing softmax and log loss
f.cross_entropy prevents numerical overflow by subtracting the max logit value before exponentiating
Mini-batch gradient descent uses approximate gradients on small batches but is more practical than full-batch optimization
Learning rate can be found by searching across exponentially-spaced values and plotting loss curves
The achieved loss of 2.3 surpasses the previous bigram model's 2.45
Datasets should be split into training (80%), validation/dev (10%), and test (10%) splits
The training split optimizes parameters, the dev split optimizes hyperparameters, and the test split evaluates final performance
Neural networks with excessive capacity relative to training data size risk memorizing the training set (overfitting)
inferredBeliefs: Karpathy believes in learning by implementing from first principles rather than using high-level APIs
He values numerical stability and efficiency in deep learning implementations
He emphasizes the importance of understanding tensor internals and memory layouts
He believes systematic hyperparameter search (learning rate range finding) is essential practice
He advocates for proper train/val/test splits as standard practice to prevent overfitting
He values showing common errors and debugging processes in educational content
He believes embeddings are a fundamental mechanism for generalizing across similar inputs
He prefers practical, incremental development over presenting finished code
workflowPatterns: Read dataset of words into a list
Build character-to-integer and integer-to-character mappings
Create training examples by sliding a context window of fixed block size over each word
Initialize embedding lookup table C with random values
Initialize hidden layer weights W1 and biases b1 randomly
Initialize output layer weights W2 and biases b2 randomly
Forward pass: embed inputs → flatten embeddings → apply hidden layer with tanh → apply output layer → compute softmax → compute cross-entropy loss
Zero gradients before each backward pass
Call loss.backward() to compute gradients
Update parameters using learning_rate × gradient descent
Use mini-batches by randomly sampling indices and indexing into dataset
Perform learning rate range search by exponentially spacing candidates and plotting loss vs learning rate
Train for multiple epochs with fixed learning rate, then decay learning rate at end of training
Evaluate loss on full training dataset to monitor actual model performance
sources.summary: Andrej Karpathy implements a multi-layer perceptron language model as an improvement over the previous bigram model, addressing the exponential growth problem by using learned embeddings and neural networks. He covers embedding lookup tables, forward/backward passes, numerical stability in cross-entropy loss, mini-batch training, and learning rate selection, while emphasizing proper train/validation/test splits to prevent overfitting.

sources.title: P6sfmUTpUmc
directClaims: We implemented the multilayer perceptron along the lines of Bengio 2003 for character level language modeling
Recurrent neural networks are universal approximators but are not easily optimizable with first order gradient based techniques
At initialization with 27 characters, we expect a loss of approximately 3.29 (negative log probability of 1/27)
The tanh activation function has a derivative of (1 - t²) where t is the output
When tanh outputs are very close to ±1, gradients vanish because (1 - (±1)²) = 0
Dead neurons occur when a neuron always fires in the flat region and never receives gradients
ReLU neurons have completely flat regions below zero that zero out gradients
The proper weight initialization scales by dividing by the square root of fan-in
The Kaiming initialization for ReLU uses a gain of √2
The Kaiming initialization for tanh uses a gain of 5/3
Batch normalization was introduced in 2015 by a team at Google
Batch normalization standardizes activations to have zero mean and unit variance
In batch normalization, scale (gain) is initialized to ones and shift (bias) to zeros
inferredBeliefs: Careful initialization is critical for neural network training, especially in deeper networks
Understanding gradient flow through activations is essential for understanding why certain architectures work or fail
Modern innovations like batch normalization and residual connections have made precise initialization less critical
Visualizing and understanding internal activations and gradients is a core debugging practice for neural networks
The behavior of neurons at initialization significantly impacts training efficiency and final performance
Gradient vanishing problems compound in deeper networks and necessitate architectural innovations
Principled initialization methods based on mathematical analysis are preferable to manual tuning
workflowPatterns: Initialize neural network weights and biases
Forward pass through embedding, linear layer, tanh activation, and output layer
Calculate loss using cross-entropy (negative log probability)
Backward pass through network to compute gradients
Update parameters using stochastic gradient descent
Visualize activation distributions using histograms to diagnose initialization problems
Check for dead neurons by examining columns of saturated activations
Apply batch normalization by computing mean and variance over batch dimension
Scale normalized activations by learnable gain and shift parameters
Evaluate model on train/val/test splits without gradient tracking
sources.summary: This lecture continues implementing neural networks for character-level language modeling, focusing on initialization and internal activation behavior. Karpathy demonstrates how improper initialization leads to vanishing gradients and saturated activations, then introduces Kaiming initialization and batch normalization as solutions to stabilize training across deeper networks.

sources.summary: This video provides practical guidance on using large language models like ChatGPT, covering model selection, pricing tiers, capabilities including thinking models and web search, and tool use. Karpathy demonstrates real-world examples of how he uses LLMs for coding problems, travel advice, medical questions, and research, while emphasizing the importance of understanding model limitations, managing context windows efficiently, and verifying outputs against primary sources.

sources.title: 7xTGNNLPyMI
directClaims: Large language models like ChatGPT are trained through a multi-stage pipeline: pre-training, neural network training, and inference
The first stage of pre-training involves downloading and processing internet text; the Fine Web dataset is approximately 44 terabytes of filtered text representing 15 trillion tokens
Common Crawl has indexed 2.7 billion web pages as of 2024 and serves as a major data source for LLM training
Data filtering stages include URL filtering (removing malware, spam, adult sites), text extraction from HTML, language filtering (Fine Web filters for >65% English), deduplication, and PII removal
Text is converted to tokens using byte pair encoding; GPT-4 uses a vocabulary of 100,277 tokens
Neural networks are trained by taking windows of tokens from datasets and predicting the next token; this is done repeatedly across billions of tokens
Modern transformer models are parameterized mathematical functions with billions of parameters that are adjusted during training to match statistical patterns in training data
GPT-2 (2019) had 1.6 billion parameters, a maximum context length of 1,024 tokens, and was trained on approximately 100 billion tokens; training cost was estimated at $40,000 in 2019
Inference works by feeding token sequences into the network, receiving probability distributions for the next token, sampling from those distributions, and repeating this process to generate text
Base models are token simulators that generate text by continuing sequences from the internet; they are not assistants and cannot answer questions directly
Llama 3.1 405 billion is a base model released by Meta trained on 15 trillion tokens
GPU clusters (like NVIDIA H100s costing $3 per GPU per hour) are essential for training large models; Nvidia's stock value is $3.4 trillion due to demand for GPUs in LLM training
The author reproduced GPT-2 for $600 in one day, compared to $40,000 in 2019, due to better datasets, faster hardware, and improved software
inferredBeliefs: Understanding the mechanics of LLMs is important for proper mental models of what these systems can and cannot do
The internet text that models train on contains biases and inaccuracies that get compressed into model parameters
Scaling up computation and data has been central to LLM improvements; bigger models with more training tokens perform better
Base models have genuine knowledge compressed in their parameters despite not being designed as assistants
The stochastic nature of token sampling is fundamental to model behavior and generates variation in outputs
workflowPatterns: Download and filter internet text → tokenize → train neural network on next-token prediction → evaluate with inference
Data preprocessing: URL filtering → HTML text extraction → language filtering → deduplication → PII removal
Training loop: sample token windows from dataset → feed into neural network → get probability predictions → calculate loss → update parameters → repeat
Inference generation: start with prefix tokens → feed to network → sample from output probability distribution → append sampled token → repeat until desired length
Model release: provide source code implementing the transformer architecture + parameter files containing the trained weights
sources.summary: This video provides a comprehensive introduction to large language models, explaining how they are built from internet text through tokenization, neural network training, and inference. Karpathy walks through the complete pipeline from data collection and filtering through transformer architecture training to generation, using concrete examples like GPT-2 and Llama 3.1 to illustrate how these systems work.

sources.title: uegyRTOrXSU

sources.summary: Cole Medin reviews over a dozen new features added to Claude Code in recent months, including 1 million token context windows, agent teams, Git worktree support, and remote control across devices. He emphasizes practical tips for when to use each feature while honestly discussing limitations, such as hallucination risks above 250-300k tokens and the experimental nature of agent teams. The video positions Claude Code as evolving into a multi-agent orchestration platform with powerful new capabilities for parallel work, automation, and memory management.

sources.summary: Cole Medin addresses exaggerated claims from tech leaders about AI replacing software engineers, examining the gap between tool capability and actual enterprise adoption. He argues that while AI coding assistants are powerful, they require strong engineering fundamentals and context-setting to be effective, and adoption across large companies remains far slower than headlines suggest. Cole presents data showing significant disconnect between enterprise licenses and daily usage, while detailing his own evolution from skeptic to advanced user of AI coding tools through his PIV loop methodology.

sources.title: qMnClynCAmM

40% of Anthropic's codebase is code for harnesses
Archon wraps above Claude Code and Codex

You can invoke multiple Archon workflows in parallel

The Archon workflow builder can automatically generate new custom workflows based on descriptions

sources.summary: Cole Medin introduces Archon, an open-source harness builder for AI coding that orchestrates multiple coding agent sessions into deterministic, repeatable workflows. He demonstrates how Archon works, showing the setup process, pre-built workflows for common tasks like fixing GitHub issues, and how to build custom workflows using YAML, while emphasizing that harness engineering is the evolution of AI development practices that can significantly improve code quality and PR acceptance rates.

sources.title: nxHKBq5ZU9U

sources.summary: Cole Medin shares the Whisk framework - a comprehensive strategy system for mastering AI coding with Claude Code based on over 2,000 hours of hands-on experience. The framework addresses context rot through four pillars: Write (externalizing agent memory via git logs and decision logs), Isolate (using sub agents for research), Select (layered context loading), and Compress (minimizing compression through proper context engineering). The video emphasizes that effective context management rather than token limits is the key to reliable AI coding agents on complex codebases.

sources.title: goOZSXmrYQ4

sources.author: Hamza Khalid
sources.text:
People think CLAUDE. md is just a notes file for Claude.

It's not.

It's the entire control system for how Claude Code thinks, acts, and stays safe inside your repo.

Here is the exact structure top engineers use:

☑ The Claude Code File Structure

claude/settings. json: committed permissions and hooks
claude/settings. local. json: gitignored, your personal overrides
claude/skills/code-review/SKILL. md: auto-triggers a code
claude/skills/testing/SKILL. md: auto-triggers your test
claude/skills/pr-description/SKILL. md: auto-writes PR
claude/skills/deploy/SKILL. md: auto-runs deploy checklist.
claude/agents/security-reviewer. md: a subagent that only reviews
claude/agents/test-writer. md: a subagent restricted to writing tests
claude/agents/docs-writer. md: a subagent restricted to writing docs
claude/agents/research. md: a subagent that reads and researches
claude/hooks. py: the script that enforces rules at compliance
claude/hooks/config/hooks-config. json: committed hook rules for repo
claude/hooks/scripts/block-sensitive-writes. sh: stop writing before start.
claude/commands/deploy. md: custom /deploy slash command.
claude/rules/architecture. md: the architecture rules claude never breaks.
gitignore: keeps your .env and secrets out of every commit.

That's why hooks are literally the only rule type that cannot fail.

If you want to go deeper, here are the exact resources I recommend:

Step 2. Subscribe to my newsletter on Substack.
Step 3. Find the high-res cheatsheet waiting inside.

sources.author: Nikki Siapno
sources.text:
REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC

𝗥𝗘𝗦𝗧 is resource-based and uses standard HTTP methods like GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE. It’s simple, widely adopted, and easy to debug, but can lead to over-fetching or under-fetching when clients need specific data.

𝗚𝗿𝗮𝗽𝗵𝗤𝗟 lets clients request exactly the data they need through a single endpoint. It reduces over-fetching and improves flexibility, but adds complexity around schema design, caching, and performance tuning.

𝗴𝗥𝗣𝗖 is a high-performance RPC framework that uses Protocol Buffers and HTTP/2. It’s efficient, strongly typed, and ideal for internal service-to-service communication, but less human-readable and harder to use from browsers.

Where they typically fit: REST is often used for public APIs, GraphQL for complex frontend data needs, and gRPC for internal service communication.

Each one shifts complexity to a different place. You’re not choosing an API, you’re choosing tradeoffs.

Full breakdown (with visuals) here → https://lnkd.in/gvrnf2Xv

What else would you add?

——

👋 PS: Want to improve at system design? Download my free System Design Handbook and join 34,000+ engineers who get my free weekly newsletter → https://lnkd.in/gueNmru7

——

♻️ Repost to help others learn system design.

sources.author: Paolo Perrone
sources.text:
The 3-hour fix that saved us $12K/month on Claude API costs.

We were burning through Opus 4.7 tokens like it was free.

Spoiler: it wasn't.

The problem?

Every API call sent the full conversation history.

50K tokens in. 50K tokens out. Every. Single. Request.

Our projected monthly bill: $14K and climbing.

The fix: sliding window context management.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘄𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗱:

→ Capped context window to last 8K tokens
→ Summarized older context into a 500-token system prompt
→ Cached summaries so we don't regenerate them every call
→ Added relevance scoring to keep only high-value context

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗹𝘁𝘀:

Before: 50K avg tokens per request
After: 9K avg tokens per request

Projected monthly cost: $14K → $2K
Quality degradation: negligible

The summary approach works because Claude doesn't need your entire chat history. It needs the relevant parts. 
Most conversations have 80% filler.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻:

1. Track token count per message
2. When context exceeds 8K, trigger summarization
3. Use a cheaper model (Haiku) to generate the summary
4. Prepend summary as system context, drop old messages
5. Cache aggressively

Total implementation time: 3 hours.
ROI: paid for itself in 4 days.

Most teams complain about inference costs.

They don't audit where the tokens actually go.

𝗧𝗲 𝘄𝗲𝗲𝗸𝗹𝘆 𝗱𝗲𝗲𝗽 𝗱𝗶𝘃𝗲:
📩 The AI Engineer → theaiengineer.substack.com
Every concept in this curriculum, from RAG to inference to agents. Free, weekly.

💾 Save this before your next Claude invoice arrives.
directClaims: Reduced API costs from $14K/month projected to $2K/month
Achieved 82% reduction in average tokens per request (50K → 9K)
Implemented sliding window context management in 3 hours
Implementation paid for itself in 4 days
Capped context window to last 8K tokens with 500-token summarized system prompt
Used cheaper model (Haiku) to generate summaries
Quality degradation was negligible after optimization

Full conversation history is unnecessary for maintaining context quality

The approach maintains quality while reducing average tokens per request from 50K to 9K by capping context windows, summarizing older messages, and using aggressive caching.

sources.author: Prashant Varshney
sources.text:
𝐖𝐡𝐢𝐜𝐡 𝐨𝐟 𝐓𝐡𝐞𝐬𝐞 𝟗 𝐋𝐚𝐲𝐞𝐫𝐬 𝐈𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐖𝐞𝐚𝐤 𝐋𝐢𝐧𝐤 𝐢𝐧 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐀𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐜 𝐑𝐀𝐆 𝐒𝐭𝐚𝐜𝐤?

Everyone's chasing precise, context-aware retrieval, the part of GenAI that actually delivers value.
But Agentic RAG done right depends on the full infrastructure behind it. Here are the 9 layers.

The foundation:

1. Deployment: Where agents are hosted and executed. Optimized compute, containerization, seamless scalability. Nothing runs without this layer working reliably.

2. Evaluation: Continuous testing, scoring, and feedback analysis measuring retrieval quality. How you know the system is actually working not just running.

The intelligence core:

3. LLMs: The thinking layer powering reasoning, conversation, planning, and decision-making. The brain of the operation but only one layer of nine.

4. Frameworks: Orchestration managing tool usage, memory access, prompt construction, agent routing. The connective tissue holding everything together.

The retrieval engine:

5. Vector Databases: Store dense vector representations for similarity-based search over large corpora. Where RAG actually retrieves from.

6. Embedding Models: Convert raw text into dense representations enabling similarity search and concept matching. The bridge between human language and vector space.

7. Data Extraction: Pipelines pulling structured and unstructured data from websites, documents, APIs. Garbage in, garbage out starts here. The quality of extraction caps everything downstream.

The persistence layer:

8. Memory: Persistent and contextual continuity across sessions. Enables personalization, long-term knowledge, adaptive behavior. What makes an agent feel less like a goldfish.

The trust layer:

9. Alignment and Observability: Tracks performance, monitors outputs, ensures safety, aligns behavior with expectations through validation and telemetry. The layer that makes everything else trustworthy.

Do you need all nine on day one?

No. They're a starting point, not a checklist. The skill is picking what your use case actually needs, integrating thoughtfully, and iterating continuously.

A team that bolts on all nine before having a working retrieval loop is over-engineering. A team that ignores evaluation and observability is flying blind. The art is knowing which layers your specific problem demands right now and which can wait.

Start with the retrieval core (embeddings, vector DB, LLM). Add evaluation early so you can measure. Layer in memory, observability, and data extraction as the use case grows.

Which layer does your stack handle well and which is the gap?

♻️ Repost this to help your network get started
➕ Follow Prashant Varshney for more

sources.author: Anastasiia S.
sources.text:
I mapped 16 ML algorithms across 8 decision factors so you never have to guess again.

Most people treat algorithm choice as a knowledge problem.

It's a constraint problem.

The cheatsheet below isn't a menu you order the best item from. It's an elimination filter. Before accuracy ever enters the conversation, three questions quietly kill most of your options:

→ How much labeled data do you actually have?
 Small data eliminates neural networks, Transformers, and Gradient Boosting before you start. That's why Naive Bayes and KNN still ship in 2026. They work when you have almost nothing to train on.

→ Do you have to explain the decision?
 A loan denial, a fraud flag, a medical triage. The moment a human has to defend the output, you lose Random Forest, Boosting, and deep nets, not because they're worse, but because "the model said so" is not an answer a regulator accepts. Logistic Regression and Decision Trees survive here for one reason: you can read them.

→ What's your latency and compute budget?
 Real-time scoring eliminates KNN (no training phase means it does all the work at prediction time) and Hierarchical Clustering (memory-intensive by design). Constraints decide this, not capability.

Only after those three filters do the trade-offs on the sheet start to matter:

☑ Linear and Logistic Regression assume a straight-line world. Fast and readable, useless the moment your data bends.

☑ Decision Tree is the most interpretable and the most unstable. Random Forest trades that interpretability away to buy stability.

☑ Gradient Boosting gives you state-of-the-art accuracy and bills you in tuning hours.

☑ Transformers win on long context and lose on everything cheap.

Here's the uncomfortable part.

The most powerful model is almost never the right one. Capability is the easiest axis to optimize, so it's the one beginners fixate on. Senior engineers optimize the opposite direction: the simplest model that clears the bar, because simple models are cheaper to run, easier to debug, and survive contact with messy production data.

That's why the column everyone skips, "When Not to Use," is the one that actually separates the two.

Accuracy is the last thing you tune.
Constraints are the first thing you check.

Learn how to train AI to write exactly like you using the voiceprint here: https://lnkd.in/d3VE4dzm

What's the last time you shipped a simpler model than the one you wanted to?

sources.author: Nikki Siapno
sources.text:
MCP vs A2A vs ACP vs ANP vs AG-UI vs AP2

To understand modern AI systems, you need to understand how these pieces fit together.

These protocols aren't competing for the same job, each one handles a different layer of how agents actually get work done:

1. 𝗠𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝘅𝘁 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝘁𝗼𝗰𝗼𝗹 (𝗠𝗖𝗣) 
↳ Connects an agent to outside tools and data through a standard client–server interface, so you skip custom code for every integration.

2. 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝟮𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 (𝗔𝟮𝗔) 
↳ Lets agents publish their capabilities, discover other agents, and exchange tasks, messages, and artifacts.

3. 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗺𝘂𝗻𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝘁𝗼𝗰𝗼𝗹 (𝗔𝗖𝗣) 
↳ Gives agents a lightweight, HTTP-native format for sending tasks, context, and responses through callable endpoints.

4. 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗡𝗲𝘁𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝘁𝗼𝗰𝗼𝗹 (𝗔𝗡𝗣)
↳ Lets agents find each other across an open network, agree on the rules, and exchange encrypted messages.

5. 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗨𝘀𝗲𝗿 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝘁𝗼𝗰𝗼𝗹 (𝗔𝗚-𝗨𝗜)
↳ Standardizes communication between the frontend UI and the agent backend, streaming updates out to the user and sending events back in real time.

6. 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗣𝗮𝘆𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝘁𝗼𝗰𝗼𝗹 (𝗔𝗣𝟮) 
↳ Adds a standardized payment layer so agents can authorize, verify, and execute payments on behalf of users.

The key idea: these protocols don’t all solve the same problem.

Some connect agents to tools.
Some connect agents to other agents.
Some connect agents to users, networks, or payment systems.

Together, they turn isolated AI models into systems that can interact with the outside world.

What else would you add?

——

👋 PS: Want to improve at AI engineering? Starting this week, our newsletter is now visually explaining AI engineering alongside system design. Join 34,000+ engineers who get my free weekly newsletter → https://lnkd.in/gueNmru7

——

♻️ Repost to help others learn AI Engineering.
➕ Follow me ( Nikki Siapno ) + turn on notifications.

sources.author: Anastasiia S.
sources.text:
A 30-message Claude session burns 232,000 tokens.
Most of it is Claude re-reading what you already typed.

Here is the part nobody explains:

Every message you send, Claude re-reads the entire conversation from the top.
Message 1 is cheap.
Message 30 means re-reading 29 exchanges before it even thinks about your actual question.

That is why your credits vanish by lunch.

Not the plan. The chat you refuse to close.

Here is what actually saves tokens:

- Edit your message instead of sending a follow-up. The old exchange gets replaced, not stacked.
- Fix one section, not the whole thing. "Only redo section 3, keep the rest."
- Batch tasks into one message. Three prompts equal three full reloads.
- Convert files to text before uploading. One PDF page is 1,500 to 3,000 tokens.
- New topic equals new chat. Old messages are dead weight Claude keeps re-reading.
- Match the model to the task. Sonnet and Haiku for light work, Opus for deep work.

What nobody talks about:

→ Long chats are token furnaces.
→ One developer tracked his usage and found 98.5% of tokens went to re-reading history. Only 1.5% reached the actual output.

Shorter context equals lower cost.

Every habit traces back to that one idea.

I put all 23 habits on one cheat sheet (in the image). Save it.

_______________________
We're teaching how to Stop Hitting Your Claude Limits for Free here: 
https://lnkd.in/dC_qi_rP
_______________________

sources.author: Stanislav Beliaev
sources.text:
A dev got tired of his AI agent turning 5-line problems into 50-line pull requests. So he built a skill to stop it:

It's called Ponytail, and it makes your coding agent reach for the simplest thing that works. Ask for a date picker and most agents install a library, write a wrapper, add a stylesheet, and debate timezones. Ponytail makes the agent write <input type="date"> and move on.

The trick: before writing anything, it looks for a reason not to. Does this need to exist? Can stdlib or a native feature handle it? Can it be one line? It stops at the first answer that holds.

On the repo's benchmark, median across Haiku, Sonnet, and Opus:
→ 80-94% less code
→ 47-77% cheaper
→ 3-6× faster

It stays lazy where laziness is free and careful where it counts. Validation, data-loss handling, security, and accessibility never get cut. Every shortcut it does take leaves a ‘ponytail:’ comment naming the upgrade path, so the debt stays findable.

Works with 13 agents. MIT licensed.

Link to the repo 👉 https://lnkd.in/dtmEEx5m

--
P.S. We’re building the best AI English tutor in the world. Try it to improve your speaking - it’s 15× cheaper than a human one → GetFluently.app

The tool achieves significant reductions in code volume, cost, and execution time while maintaining quality standards and tracking technical debt.

sources.author: Hao Hoang
sources.text:
Massachusetts Institute of Technology just made its entire AI/ML book library free.

Twelve textbooks. The actual foundations. Zero paywall.

And most engineers who save this post will never open one of them.

I've watched this play out for years. Free was never the thing standing between you and understanding deep learning. Finishing was.

The honest math on a list like this:
→ You'll bookmark all 12
→ You'll open maybe 2
→ You'll finish 0

Not because the books are hard. Because "I'll read it later" is where free resources go to die. A $200 course you paid for gets finished. A free PDF gets a tab that stays open for six months.

So here's how I'd actually use these, if I were starting over:

1️⃣ Pick ONE based on the gap you have right now, not the one that looks most impressive.
2️⃣ Read 10 pages. Implement one idea from those 10 pages in code today
3️⃣ Only then earn the next book

The list everyone's sharing (full thing in comments):
Foundations of ML · Understanding Deep Learning · the Goodfellow Deep Learning · Sutton & Barto RL · Probabilistic ML · Fairness and ML · and 6 more.

All world-class. All free. All useless if they stay in a tab.

It was also kind of fun to beat the status quo (actually spank it), but I have the benefit of an absolutely incredible network around me that supported me and gave me tough love when I needed it. Authenticity, grit, and putting in the work can still win the day!

I was also fortunate to anticipate a larger wave in the city and grab my surfboard.

Thanks to my neighbors, my family, my fellow committee leaders, and the friends virtual and corporeal who gave me a boost.

sources.author: Paolo Perrone
sources.text:
Karpathy's LLM workflow went viral. I've been running an advanced version since mid-2025.

1,400 records. 440 signals. Producing content for NVIDIA, Google, CodeRabbit, and MongoDB.

Here's what 9 months in production taught me that a demo can't:

Obsidian is great for notes. It's terrible for memory.

1️⃣ You can't query markdown
"Show me every post that scored above 50 and ran on a Wednesday."
In a database: one query. Milliseconds.
In Obsidian: read every file. Hope Claude catches it. Pray the formatting is consistent across 9 months of entries.
At 50 notes this works. At 1,400 records it's burning tokens on irrelevant context every session.

2️⃣ You can't traverse relationships
"Which topics perform best with which audience segments?"
A database traverses this in one query.
Obsidian's wikilinks look like relationships. They're not. You can't query the structure. A folder of linked files is not a graph database any more than sticky notes on a wall are a relational schema.

3️⃣ No schema enforcement
Monday: Claude formats a source as ## Source Name - URL
Tuesday: **Source Name** (URL)
Wednesday: Source, see link
Good luck parsing that programmatically. Good luck even finding all three entries when you search.

4️⃣ Scale kills you silently
My signal library: 440 data points across 18 categories. Dumping that into context every session is expensive and crowds out actual work. Every token spent on "memory" is a token not available for the task.

5️⃣ Multiple agents break everything
I run scoring, sequencing, and scheduling agents on the same data. Two agents writing the same markdown file simultaneously = silent data corruption. Databases handle concurrent access natively. Markdown doesn't.

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗜 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝘂𝘀𝗲:

Airtable for structured production data. Scored, sequenced, queryable.
Project knowledge files for instructions.
Memory system for cross-session context.
Signal library derived from 1,200+ posts and comments analyzed.

The .md files in my system contain instructions.
The databases contain the knowledge.

CLAUDE.md is a config file. Not a brain.

It started in Obsidian. It didn't stay there.
Your system shouldn't either.

20 minutes of database setup saves 6 months of workarounds.

Are you still storing your AI's "memory" in markdown files? 👇

💾 Bookmark this before your next "AI second brain" build.
♻️ Repost for someone building their "AI second brain" on sticky notes.
directClaims: Running an advanced version of Karpathy's LLM workflow since mid-2025
Processing 1,400 records with 440 signals for NVIDIA, Google, CodeRabbit, and MongoDB
You can't query markdown files efficiently at scale
Obsidian wikilinks are not queryable as a graph database
No schema enforcement in markdown leads to inconsistent formatting
Dumping 440 signal data points into context every session is token-expensive
Multiple agents writing to the same markdown file causes silent data corruption
Using Airtable for structured production data instead of markdown
20 minutes of database setup saves 6 months of workarounds

sources.author: Alex Xu
sources.text:
Polling vs Long Polling vs Webhooks vs SSE

Four ways to get updates from a server. Each one makes a different tradeoff between simplicity, efficiency, and real-time delivery.

Here's how they compare:

- Polling: The client sends a request every few seconds asking "anything new?" The server responds immediately, whether or not there's new data. Most of those requests come back empty, wasting client and server resources. For use cases like an order status page where a small delay is acceptable, polling is the simplest option to implement.

- Long Polling: The client sends a request, and the server keeps the HTTP connection open until new data is available or a timeout occurs. This means fewer empty responses compared to regular polling. Some chat applications used this pattern to deliver messages closer to real-time communication.

- Server-Sent Events (SSE): The client opens a persistent HTTP connection, and the server streams events through it as they're generated. It is one-way, lightweight, and built on plain HTTP. Many AI responses that appear token by token are delivered through SSE, streaming each chunk over a single open connection.

- Webhooks: Instead of the client asking for updates, the service sends an HTTP POST to a pre-registered callback URL whenever a specific event occurs. Stripe uses this for payment confirmations. GitHub uses it for push events. The client never polls or holds a connection open, it just waits for the server to call.

Many systems don't rely on a single pattern. You may use polling for order status, SSE for streaming AI responses, and webhooks for payment confirmations.

--

AI coding: more value is starting to show up in process design.

I liked this repo for that reason.

It breaks software work into agent-native stages like 
/spec
/plan
/build
/test
/review
/ship

Not especially because the commands themselves are new.

More because it reflects where a lot of AI coding still breaks:

not at raw generation,
but at specification, sequencing, verification, and review.

A lot of agent output still fails in very unglamorous ways.
Wrong assumption.
Too much surface area changed at once.
Weak testing.
No clear quality boundary before merge.

This is why the more interesting AI coding setups are starting to look less like “one smart prompt”
and more like workflow systems with structure.

Repo by Addy Osmani, link in comments.

sources.author: GenAI Works
sources.text:
9 ways stop hitting Claude's usage limit:

1. Shrink Your Files:

PDFs and screenshots eat tons of credits. Copy-paste the text into a plain file instead. Crop images to only what matters. 
Tip: Smaller file = fewer credits burned.

2. Plan First. Build Last:

Don't start with "make me a spreadsheet." Use Chat to figure out what you want. Once it's clear, then build the file. 
Tip: Think cheap. Build expensive.

3. Say "Ask Me Questions":

Stop writing 500-word prompts. 
Add: "Ask me questions before you start." Then click the options Claude gives you. 
Tip: Clicking costs almost nothing. Typing costs a lot.

4. Edit, Don't Resend.

Made a mistake? Don't send a new message. Hit Edit on your old one. Fix it. Regenerate. The old version gets replaced, not stacked. 
Tip: Every "no wait, I meant…" makes your chat more expensive.

5. Start Fresh Every 15–20 Messages:

Long chats get expensive fast. Every new message re-reads the entire history. After 15–20 messages: ask for a summary. Copy it. Start a new chat. Paste it in. 
Tip: Short chats = cheap chats.

6. Pick the Right Model:

Easy stuff → Haiku (fast, cheap). Writing or reports → Opus (smart, costly). Code or charts → Sonnet (middle ground). 
Tip: Don't use a bulldozer to plant a flower.

7. Use Projects for Recurring Files:

Stop uploading the same file to every chat. Put it in a Project once. Every chat inside that Project can see it. 
Tip: Upload once. Use forever.

8. Turn Off What You Don't Need:

Web search, connectors, extended thinking — all use credits. Even when you don't need them. If you're just writing? Turn them off. 
Tip: Only pay for what you're using right now.

9. Batch + Spread Your Day:

Don't burn everything in one morning. Claude resets on a rolling 5-hour window. Work in 2–3 sessions across the day.
Put 3 tasks in 1 message, not 3 messages. 
Tip: By the time you're back, your limit has rolled off.

Have you hit Claude's usage limit before? Which of these fixes would have saved you?

sources.text:
If you're not using Claude Skills, you're wasting your time.

Claude Skills is the most underrated claude feature, because it can be too technical.

7 easy Claude skills hack that help you save a lot of time:

▪️Hack 1: Use the Skill Creator Open Cowork. Select your folder. Switch to Opus 4.6 + Extended Thinking.

Type: "Use the skill-creator to help me build a skill for [your most repeated task]." It interviews you. It builds everything.

▪️Hack 2: Write Better Negative Triggers Your Skill fires when it shouldn't, you ask a simple question and the wrong Skill activates.

The fix: the "Do NOT use for…" line matters more than the "Use when…" line. 80% of a good Skill is defining what it's NOT for. Most people only write what it IS for. That's why it breaks.

▪️Hack 3: Stack Skills with your Voice File Your about-me.md tells Claude who you are. Your Skill tells Claude how to do the job. 
They fire together, simultaneously. Your LinkedIn Skill doesn't need your tone rules. Claude already knows your voice from the .md file.

▪️Hack 4: Build Skills from Past Chats Don't start from scratch. You've been giving Claude instructions for months, those old prompts already contain the process.

Click on a Cowork chat session, click the name's arrow, turn it into a Skill. Claude reverse-engineers your workflow. Done.

▪️Hack 5: Skills Save Tokens You'd think 20 installed Skills eat your usage. It's the opposite.

Claude only reads the 3-line header of each Skill. Full instructions load only when a task matches. A task that took 12,000 tokens without Skills? 2 messages. 6,000 tokens. With one.

▪️Hack 6: The Debugging Trick Your Skill doesn't fire and you don't know why. Prompt: "When would you use [skill-name] skill?" Claude quotes the exact description back to you.

You instantly see what's vague, what's missing. Fastest fix for any Skill that won't activate.

▪️Hack 7: Skills are Portable Anthropic published Skills as an open standard. The same SKILL.md file works across platforms.

How are you using your Claude skills?

Want to stop re-explaining tasks and make Claude actually work like a teammate? That’s exactly what we cover in our Claude course:

sources.author: 🦾Jepson Taylor
sources.text:
Humans don't understand hiveminds... because we have two eyes... and 10 fingers... and 10 toes... that makes sense. Unlike an Elephant we can't imagine controlling 10,000 muscles in a trunk. Unlike a Dolphin we can't comprehend sonar. In the case of AI we can't comprehend a hivemind, one single entity controlling multiple agents (50 fingers)... all experience what all experience... coordinating as one mind in real-time, a fun RL problem.

Knowing this can bring about some interesting behaviors (e.g. self sacrifice for the greater good, etc..)

In the near future every drone, droid, remote agent will be operated by a hive that understands each agent's roll in a bigger picture. DeepMind made RL famous when sold to Google, I think Generative RL will grow in interest in the coming years. No RL algorithm IMO deployed should have human origin, if it does it is leaving value on the table. The smartest humans in the world could build algorithms for the blue team, and they would easily be beaten by the red team with engineered intelligence (AI inventing better algorithms in realtime). RL decomposition has not been done yet, but if you can do it with an LLM you can easily do it for RL. (video two hive minds competing, getting smarter with an infinitely evolving worthy opponent).

sources.author: Aniket Hingane
sources.text:
The Simple Must-Have Guide for AGENTS.md: Revolutionizing AI-Driven Development Workflows

Full Article : https://lnkd.in/gDaHzHUy

Transform your development process with the standardized specification that’s changing how AI agents collaborate with human developers

➡️ What is this article about

This comprehensive guide explores AGENTS.md, an emerging open specification that’s revolutionizing how artificial intelligence agents interact with software development workflows. We’ll dive deep into understanding what AGENTS.md is, how it works, and why it’s becoming an essential tool for modern development teams who want to harness the power of AI-driven automation.

AGENTS.md represents a paradigm shift from traditional documentation approaches. While README.md files serve humans well, they often leave AI agents confused about exact procedures, dependencies, and execution order. AGENTS.md bridges this gap by providing a machine-readable, standardized format that both humans and AI agents can interpret with precision.

Throughout this guide, we’ll explore the technical implementation, examine real-world use cases, and provide you with practical examples you can implement immediately in your own projects. Whether you’re a seasoned developer looking to integrate AI agents into your workflow or a team lead exploring automation possibilities, this guide will equip you with the knowledge to leverage AGENTS.md effectively.

➡️ Why Read It

The software development landscape is undergoing a fundamental transformation. AI coding assistants and autonomous agents are no longer experimental tools — they’re becoming integral parts of development teams. However, the lack of standardized communication protocols between humans and AI agents has created chaos in many organizations.

Consider the typical scenario many development teams face today. You have a complex Python project with multiple dependencies, testing frameworks, linting tools, and deployment procedures. When a new team member joins, they spend hours deciphering README files, hunting down missing dependencies, and figuring out the correct sequence of commands to get everything working. Now imagine trying to communicate these same procedures to an AI agent that lacks human intuition and context understanding.

This is where AGENTS.md becomes invaluable. By reading this guide, you’ll understand how to create clear, unambiguous instructions that both human developers and AI agents can follow reliably. You’ll learn to eliminate the friction that occurs when transitioning between manual and automated development processes.

sources.author: Aniket Hingane
sources.text:
How I Built An Open-Source Crop Layout Verifier for Precision Agriculture

Full Article : https://lnkd.in/g9RBHNJz

The AI Eye on the Field: Engineering a System to Eliminate Planting Errors

➡️ TL;DR
I built a System that acts like an automated quality inspector for agriculture. It analyzes a photo of a seedling tray to check if plants are planted correctly. First, it uses color detection to find all the green plants, ignoring the soil and plastic tray.

Then, based on where it finds the plants, it mathematically determines the ideal grid pattern for that specific tray.

Finally, it compares the actual plant locations to this ideal grid and generates a simple report, highlighting which plants are correctly placed, which spots are empty (missing), and which plants are off-center (misplaced). This tool helps farmers quickly and automatically spot planting errors that could impact their crop yield.

➡️ My Journey And Learning
In my journey to build what seemed like a simple AI agent — a “Crop Layout Verifier” — I navigated a minefield of common but often-undiscussed computer vision challenges. The initial idea was to use basic image processing to check if plants were arranged correctly in a seedling tray. This seemingly straightforward task quickly unraveled.

My first attempt, using grayscale thresholding, failed completely on a real-world, poorly lit image. My second attempt, using a more robust HSV color segmentation method, led to an unexpected Python crash (ValueError: cannot convert float NaN to integer) due to brittle logic in the pattern analysis. After fixing the crash, the system still failed, tricked by finding incorrect, small shapes instead of the main tray.

The final, successful solution required a multi-layered approach: a robust color-based detection pipeline, resilient pattern analysis logic, and critical validation checks to prevent the system from deceiving itself. This article is the complete, unfiltered story of that process, detailing every bug, every "aha!" moment, and every line of code that took this project from a frustrating failure to a remarkable success.

➡️Introduction: The Lego Inspector and the X-Ray Machine
Imagine you’re a quality control inspector at a Lego factory. Your one job is to ensure that every single Lego set that leaves the assembly line is perfect. Specifically, you have to check the transparent plastic trays inside the box, the ones with dozens of little compartments, to make sure that a tiny, green Lego piece is in each designated spot.

sources.author: Edward Newton
sources.text:
The myth of the lone genius is killing us creatively.

I've been working in entertainment since 2007, in one form or another. The problem of "how do I break through the noise" is not a new one.

I see the same things happening in games that I did in film and TV: people investing hundreds/thousands of hours into something in private, and then standing there, shocked, when no one shows up to the release party.

"But I sweated for years! I worked in silence and committed everything to this, just like they said the greats did it!"

It did not occur to them that they should put their work in front of someone before they put that much work into it.

"But, but, but it's not DONE!"

That really doesn't matter as much as you think it does.

All that matters is that it moves somebody.

And the only way to know is to put it out there and see if it moves someone.

If it does, work on it a little more and see if people respond more. If it doesn't, move on to the next idea.

You are not a AAA studio, you don't have shareholders breathing down your neck for product in Q2. Pack light, iterate fast, and keep trying new ideas. Quantity is, by far, better than quality when you're looking for a new idea.

"But it's not polished, nobody will like it!"

Don't coddle your ideas. Let them stand on their own.

A game that can't rise above its lack of polish doesn't deserve to be polished.
directClaims: The myth of the lone genius is killing us creatively
People invest hundreds/thousands of hours into something in private, then are shocked when no one shows up to release
It did not occur to them that they should put their work in front of someone before investing that much work
All that matters is that it moves somebody
Quantity is, by far, better than quality when you're looking for a new idea
A game that can't rise above its lack of polish doesn't deserve to be polished
inferredBeliefs: Perfectionism is a barrier to creative success
Early feedback and iteration are more valuable than secretive polish
Independent creators should prioritize learning over finished products
Market validation through release matters more than internal completion standards
Risk-averse creative practices lead to wasted effort and missed opportunities
sources.summary: Edward Newton argues that creative professionals, particularly game developers, waste effort pursuing perfection in isolation rather than releasing early work to get market feedback. He advocates for rapid iteration and quantity over quality when exploring new ideas.

sources.author: 🦾Jepson Taylor
sources.text:
One of our agents delivered a new record on our internal optimization benchmarks. The reason we hash our algorithms is they are being named internally by the agent. This one is the “AuroraDragon”, currently the highest benchmarking optimizers we have access to. Don't get too attached emotionally, there will always be a better one tomorrow, same is true for generative machine learning algorithms being discovered right now. If you had a billion dollar prize for humans to deliver this type of innovation, they could not using traditional methods. When it comes to algorithm development the human has stalled in comparison.

Something that is kind of fun, I've been doing this for awhile, giving code to image generators and asking them to imagine the code as a creature. Well, this is that new creature.

A funny thing, on the horizon is autonomous white paper generation. So when a new algorithm is invented, sure a human could spend weeks writing a white paper to share with the world, but so can an agent... It's a bit complicated, but completely possible, that you will soon see a flood of white papers that are done with more rigor than the typical researcher. You won't know if they are coming from very smart humans, or smarter agents. A few months ago an academic pushed back on LLMs writing white papers. Sorry not sorry, I have one speed and it's faster than before.

sources.author: Carly Taylor
sources.text:
This feels crazy to write, but… I’ve made the difficult decision to leave Call of Duty.

I started working there almost 5 years ago as a senior data scientist. I hired my first engineer shortly after that and started tackling some of the hardest problems we faced as an organization.

A LOT of hard work, long weekends, 6 global launches, and awesome wins later, I am stepping away after growing my team of 1 (me!) to a team of 40 highly technical and capable engineers.

From kernel development to machine learning, I feel like I was able to make an impact at every level in our tech stack.

It was an incredibly exciting, challenging, and rewarding journey.

I can’t wait to share with you all where I’ll be heading next, but first I want to take a moment to appreciate the awesome people who made my journey what it was.

Johanna Faries - you gave me permission to find my voice. You are the kind of leader people dream to work for, and the way you command respect through empathy, authenticity and mutual kindness should be studied. You’re the 🐐.

Stephanie Snowden - girl, I would follow you into any battle, any day. You’re one of the strongest people I know, and it was a pleasure being in the trenches with you 🫡 as long as you were around, I knew we were on the right track.

Natalya T. - probably my biggest regret is that I didn’t get to learn more from you. You have a knack for making extraordinarily difficult things look easy. From technical acumen to navigating complex human situations, you never faltered. I appreciated every bit of advice (even when it seemed like I wasn’t listening).

Thank you to everyone who followed my journey with COD! It was the most fun I’ve ever had, and I can’t wait to share what’s next (guesses are welcome 😆)

GLHF ✌️

sources.author: Vaibhav Srivastav
sources.text:
HOLY SHITT, Microsoft dropped an open-source Multimodal (supports Audio, Vision and Text) Phi 4 - MIT licensed! 🔥

> Beats Gemini 2.0 Flash, GPT4o, Whisper, SeamlessM4T v2
> Models on Hugging Face hub, integrated with/ Transformers!

Phi-4-Multimodal:

> Modalities: Integrates text, vision, and speech/audio
> Architecture: Uses "Mixture of LoRAs" to add modality-specific adapters without fine-tuning the base model
> Vision Modality: SigLIP-400M image encoder, 2-layer MLP projector, dynamic multi-crop strategy
> Speech/Audio Modality: 3-layer convolution, 24 conformer blocks, 80ms token rate
> Performance: Ranks first on OpenASR leaderboard, supports vision+language, vision+speech, and speech/audio tasks, outperforming larger models

Phi-4-Mini:

> Parameters: 3.8 billion
> Architecture: 32 Transformer layers, 3,072 hidden state size, Group Query Attention (GQA) with 24 query heads and 8 key/value heads
> Vocabulary: 200K tokens for multilingual support.
Training Data: High-quality web and synthetic data, emphasizing math and coding
> Performance: Outperforms similar-sized models and matches larger models (e.g., DeepSeek-Rl-Distill-Qwen-7B) on math and coding tasks

Training Pipeline:

> Language Training: Pre-training on 5 trillion tokens, post-training with function calling, summarization, and instruction-following data
> Multimodal Training: Vision training (4 stages), speech/audio training (2 stages), and joint vision-speech training
> Reasoning Training: Pre-trained on 60B CoT tokens, fine-tuned on 200K high-quality CoT samples, and DPO-trained on 300K preference samples

Vision Benchmarks:
> Outperforms Phi-3.5-Vision, Qwen2.5-VL, InternVL2.5, and matches Gemini and GPT-4o on tasks like chart understanding and OCR
> Vision-Speech Benchmarks: Significantly outperforms InternOmni and Gemini-2.0-Flash

Speech Benchmarks:

> ASR: Achieves SOTA on CommonVoice, FLEURS, and Open ASR Leaderboard, surpassing WhisperV3 and SeamlessM4T
> AST: Best performance on CoVoST2, comparable to GPT-4o on FLEURS
> Speech Summarization: First open-source model with this capability, close to GPT-4o in quality

Language Benchmarks:

> Outperforms similar-sized models (Llama-3.2, Ministral) and matches larger models (Qwen2.5-7B) on math, reasoning, and coding tasks
> Coding: Strong performance on HumanEval, MBPP, and BigCodeBench

Reasoning Benchmarks:

> Reasoning-enhanced Phi-4-Mini outperforms DeepSeek-Rl-Distill-Llama-8B and matches DeepSeek-Rl-Distill-Qwen-7B on AIME, MATH-500, and GPQA Diamond

sources.text:
🚀 Introducing Woz - Your AI Technical Cofounder 🚀

Brad Eckert and I are excited to announce that Woz (YC W25) has been accepted to Y Combinator.

Woz is an AI platform that enables non technical entrepreneurs to build and scale real software businesses - eliminating the need for a technical cofounder or developer team.

Historically, the ability to write code and architect software served as a massive barrier to business building. Woz eliminates this barrier with a team of AI agents that enable anyone - regardless of technical ability - to build products, go to market, and grow a business all on one platform.

Our vision is simple: eliminate barriers to business building and make entrepreneurship accessible to all. As AI automates traditional industries and employers reduce headcount, the ability to start and own businesses will become even more important. Woz makes this possible.

We’re incredibly fortunate to be joined by a world-class team of ML scientists and production engineers and to be backed by bold investors - Y Combinator, MGV.VC, Untapped Ventures and more.

✨ If you’ve been itching to launch that mobile app, we’d love to hear from you! Drop us a message or apply for Woz Beta (link in comments).

We can’t wait to see what you build! 🚀

sources.author: Leonie Monigatti
sources.text:
I think this is pretty interesting:

Fine-tuning a model for function calling.

When it comes to building agent systems, we often talk about which massive model is the most powerful at reasoning.

But sometimes, you might not need a model that excels at reasoning.
Sometimes, you might just want a model that excels at calling functions.

In this bonus unit of Hugging Face's Agents course, you'll learn 
- how to fine-tune Gemma 2 2b IT 
- using the hermes-function-calling-v1 dataset by NousResearch
- using LoRA fine-tuning
- and all within 6 hours in Google Colab with the free tier T4 GPU.

Pretty cool.

Here's the link to the notebook: 
https://lnkd.in/gtMqzwh8

LoRA fine-tuning can be completed in 6 hours in Google Colab with free tier T4 GPU
For agent systems, you might not need a model that excels at reasoning, but rather one that excels at calling functions
